AST-4.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and accuracy of the authoritative asset inventory: percentage of all assets (by type: hardware, software, data, personnel) recorded in the inventory, percentage of assets with assigned owner and sensitivity/criticality classification, frequency of inventory updates, and count of discrepancies discovered per audit cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control verifying coverage and correctness (coverage % of assets, % with owners/classification). Counts (e.g., unrecorded assets, discrepancies) and trends (coverage over time) supplement the percentage to show volume and trajectory.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is percent coverage of the authoritative inventory: numerator = assets recorded with unique asset ID, owner, type and sensitivity tag; denominator = total discovered assets from automated discovery + validated manual inventory. Also measure: timestamp of last update per asset, count of inventory-audit discrepancies, and percent of assets with assigned owner and with classified sensitivity.
data_signals: asset discovery scan results with timestamps and asset identifiers
authoritative asset inventory/CMDB records (asset id, type, classification, owner, last_updated)
asset provisioning and decommissioning logs (create/decommission timestamps)
reconciliation/audit reports listing discrepancies and counts
terminated-employee equipment return logs (asset id, return status)
percentage of assets with sensitivity/criticality tags
list of assets missing owner or classification
frequency of inventory updates per asset (update timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated discovery feeds reconciling to an authoritative CMDB with timestamped updates and assigned owners; regular reconciliation/audit reports showing low discrepancy counts.
Moderate: periodic automated scans plus manual updates where timestamps and owner fields exist but reconciliation is infrequent.
Weak: one-off spreadsheets or manual inventories without discovery logs, missing owner/classification fields, or no audit reconciliation history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control describes identifying, classifying and ensuring assets are accounted for (preventing untracked assets and associated risks) which is a preventive posture.

AST-4.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of inventory completeness, ownership assignment, classification accuracy and reconciliation cadence — unlike sibling controls that may focus on protection or remediation, AST-4 is uniquely about coverage and record accuracy.

AST-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of assets in the authoritative asset register that have a designated, current owner and track counts of unowned or overdue-reviewed assets by asset type (data, hardware, software, IP), plus the frequency of asset-inventory updates and owner reassessments.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage (owners assigned to assets) so percentage (coverage %) is primary; counts of unowned assets and trends over time are useful secondary metrics to show remediation and review frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute total assets in the authoritative asset register and the number with a non-null, current owner_id; calculate percentage with owners by asset_type (hardware, software, data, IP), report last_updated_timestamp distribution, count ownership assignment or change events during the reporting period, and count assets outstanding against HR termination/return records.
data_signals: asset_register records (asset_id, asset_type, owner_id, classification, last_updated_timestamp)
owner_assignment audit logs (asset_id, previous_owner_id, new_owner_id, assigner_id, timestamp, reason)
asset_inventory_update execution timestamps and source (automated sync, manual update)
owner training and awareness completion records (owner_id, training_id, completion_date)
HR termination records linked to asset issue/return (employee_id, asset_id, issued_date, return_status, return_date)
asset lifecycle events (decommission, transfer, disposal entries with timestamps and new_owner_id)
periodic review/audit reports indicating reassessment outcomes and exceptions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative, automated asset registry with non-null owner_id for >95% of assets, timestamped ownership assignment history, and ongoing reconciliation against HR termination/returns.
Moderate: periodic audit reports and scheduled inventory update logs showing improvement but gaps in automation or timestamps.
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets or a single point-in-time inventory without assignment history or linkage to HR/ownership change events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly labels this as a 'preventive control' and emphasizes assignment of owners and proactive inventory management.

AST-5.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that may focus on protection controls or disposal, this control specifically requires measurement of owner assignment coverage, inventory update frequency, owner training completion, and reconciliation with HR termination/asset-return records.

AST-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of organizational assets (hardware, software, media) assigned to departing or transferring personnel that are returned within the defined timeline and verified as sanitized, plus the count of outstanding/unreturned assets and incidents tied to unreturned assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires verification of coverage and timeliness (e.g., percent of assets returned on time and sanitized), so percentage is primary; counts of unreturned assets, return rates, and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show volume and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (a) percent of assets assigned to terminated/transferred employees returned within policy SLA (by asset type and criticality), (b) count of assets not returned after SLA expiry, (c) count/percentage of returned assets with sanitization/ERASURE evidence, and (d) number of incidents or breaches linked to unreturned or unsanitized assets over the reporting period.
data_signals: asset inventory records with current assignee and asset ID
onboarding/termination/change of employment records with timestamps
asset assignment and return receipt timestamps
exit interview / exit checklist completion records
device sanitization / data-wipe logs or certificates of destruction
audit reconciliation reports listing inventory discrepancies and overdue returns
incident records linking breaches to missing/unreturned assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset management logs showing assignment and return timestamps plus device sanitization certificates and audit reconciliation with zero discrepancies.
Strong: signed return receipts or system-logged change-of-ownership events correlated to termination timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc manual spreadsheets or verbal confirmations without sanitization logs.
Weak: annual-only inventory snapshots without per-termination reconciliation, which do not prove timely returns.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is framed to 'ensure' assets are returned promptly and securely and prescribes policies, timelines and procedures, which are preventive measures to avoid data breaches and unauthorized access.

AST-6.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the return, reconciliation and sanitization of assets at termination/transfer (timeliness and proof of data removal), unlike sibling asset controls that may focus on inventory creation, classification, or general protection measures.

AST-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of physical and digital assets marked for retirement that are fully decommissioned and sanitized within the defined timeframe, plus the count and rate of incidents or failures related to improper asset disposal or residual data. Also measure coverage of the asset inventory (assets tracked vs discovered) and frequency of removal-process audits.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control focused on coverage and completion within timeframe (best expressed as percentages of assets sanitized/completed). Counts of failed disposals and trends over time (remediation improvement) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count assets slated for removal and compare to assets successfully decommissioned and sanitized (with verification) within the policy-defined timeframe; capture sanitization method and verification artifacts per asset; record disposal/destruction receipts or certificates; and count incidents referencing improper disposal or residual data exposure. Group measurements by asset type, owner, and criticality to validate prioritized handling (e.g., sensitive data-bearing devices).
data_signals: asset inventory records with asset_id, asset_type, owner, location, and lifecycle_status (active/retired/removed)
decommission request and completion timestamps (decommission request time, sanitization completion time)
data wiping/sanitization logs and verification artifacts (method used, NIST SP 800-88 reference, verification hash or wipe report)
media sanitization/destruction certificates or disposal receipts (vendor destruction confirmation)
audit findings for removal processes and proof-of-removal inspection records
incidents or exception logs referencing improper asset disposal or residual data exposure
counts of assets discovered by scans/inventory reconciliation that are not present in official inventory (untracked assets)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset inventory updates showing status transitions with timestamps, machine-generated wipe logs and verification artifacts, and signed disposal certificates
Moderate: documented audit reports and batch sanitization reports without per-asset verification hashes
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets, attestation emails, or screenshots without machine-verifiable sanitization artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates establishing formal processes (inventory, data wiping, secure disposal) and preventing residual data on retired assets, which is primarily preventive in nature.

AST-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on end-of-life handling (sanitization and disposal) and verification artifacts per asset, unlike sibling asset controls that may focus on identification, access, or ongoing protection; measurement must therefore include sanitization logs and disposal certificates rather than only inventory presence.

AST-8.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of assets taken off-premises that are inventoried, protected (encrypted and access-controlled), and manageable remotely, plus the count and rate of incidents (loss/theft/unauthorized access) involving off‑premises assets and the percentage of terminated employees who returned company assets on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage and compliance (inventory, encryption, training) which map to percentage metrics (coverage %, training completion %). Incident volumes and return counts are best shown as counts/rates and trends over time to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics: (1) percent of off‑premises assets present in the central asset inventory with an 'off‑premises' flag and associated owner; (2) percent of those assets with encryption_enabled = true and access_controls (strong authentication) applied; (3) MDM/remote‑management capability presence and remote_wipe_enabled flag; (4) count and rate of lost/stolen/unauthorized access incidents tied to off‑premises assets and remote‑wipe success/failure events; (5) percentage of terminated employees with asset return checklist completed within the required timeframe; (6) employee secure‑handling training completion rate for roles that take assets off‑premises.
data_signals: asset inventory records with off‑premises flag and last_known_location
device encryption_status field (full‑disk or container encryption)
access_control configuration records or strong_authentication_enforced flag per device
remote_wipe_enabled flag and remote_wipe_execution logs (timestamps, success/failure)
asset checkout/checkin timestamps and owner assignment records
incident/ticket records for lost/stolen/unauthorized access with asset IDs and outcomes
termination checklists / asset return records showing returned_assets_count and return_date
employee training completion records for secure handling of off‑premises assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset inventory and device management logs showing enrollment, encryption_status, remote_wipe events and successful wipe confirmations versus Weak: ad‑hoc spreadsheets or policy documents without machine‑readable device state
Strong: time‑stamped incident records linking asset IDs to outcomes and remediation actions versus Weak: verbal reports or unspecific incident summaries
Strong: systemed training completion records and enforced access controls versus Weak: attendance sign‑in sheets or unsigned policy acknowledgements

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes policies, protective measures (encryption, access controls), inventory and training to prevent loss/theft/unauthorized access, indicating a preventive control.

AST-8.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on assets when they leave physical premises — measurement must combine inventory/off‑premises flags, device protection state (encryption, remote‑wipe) and return/training metrics, unlike sibling asset controls that may focus only on on‑site classification or asset lifecycle registration.

AST-9.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of retired or repurposed assets that received documented, certified data destruction (e.g., degaussing, shredding, secure wipe) or secure overwrite prior to disposal/reuse, and count incidents of recoverable data from disposed equipment while tracking chain-of-custody and third‑party attestation coverage over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage verification (percentage of assets with verified destruction). Counts (incidents, disposed assets) and trends (compliance over time) are useful secondary metrics to show volume and improvement; distribution helps show methods used and gaps by asset type.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (number of retired/repurposed assets with a recorded certified destruction method and chain-of-custody entry) divided by (total number of retired/repurposed assets) per period; additionally capture count of incidents where data was recovered from disposed equipment, vendor destruction certificates, and training completion for disposal procedures.
data_signals: disposed equipment inventory records (asset tag/serial, model, asset owner, retirement date, disposition status)
data destruction report/logs from wipe tools (tool name, timestamp, verification/hash or wipe result, operator ID)
certificate of destruction from third-party vendor (vendor ID, method used, timestamp)
chain-of-custody logs (handoff timestamps, personnel/vendor IDs, transport/receipt records)
number of incidents of data recovery from disposed equipment (incident ID, date, asset tag)
asset retirement approval and authorization records (request and approval timestamps)
employee training completion records for secure disposal procedures (user ID, course, completion date)
audit findings or compliance audit results referencing disposal practices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated secure‑wipe tool logs with verification hashes, matching chain‑of‑custody entries and vendor certificates for each retired asset; regular audit reports showing full coverage.
Moderate: scanned PDF certificates and manual disposal logs for most assets with periodic sampling verification.
Weak: one-off attestations or verbal confirmations without destruction logs, missing serial numbers, or no vendor certificates; lack of chain-of-custody.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires that data be 'irretrievably destroyed or securely overwritten prior to the retirement or repurposing' of equipment, which is a preventive requirement to avoid future data exposure.

AST-9.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about end-of-life asset disposition and proof of destruction (inventory, destruction certificates, chain-of-custody), unlike sibling asset controls that may focus on identification, tracking, or access — measurement must verify destruction prior to disposal rather than just asset presence or configuration.

AST-10.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user devices configured to automatically lock after the defined inactivity period, the percentage of users completing required training on securing unattended equipment, and the count and rate of incidents involving unauthorized access to unattended devices or policy violations per audit period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily preventive (requires coverage/configuration and policy adherence), so percentage metrics (device configuration coverage, training completion rate) best show compliance; counts and rates are useful secondary measures for incident occurrences and audit findings, and trends show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) total managed user devices and the subset with a screen-lock configured to the policy-defined inactivity timeout (to produce a device auto-lock coverage percentage); (2) number and percentage of users who have completed unattended-device security training; (3) count and timestamps of incidents or reports of unauthorized access to unattended devices and counts of audit findings related to unlocked devices; and (4) exceptions or waiver records for devices/areas deemed high-risk.
data_signals: endpoint configuration records showing screen lock timeout setting and enforcement status
inventory of managed user devices with device identifiers and location/high-risk area tag
endpoint management policy application logs (profile push success/failure timestamps)
authentication/session logs indicating unlocked workstation periods and manual lock events
incident reports and ticket records flagged as 'unauthorized access' or 'unattended device' with timestamps
user training completion records and timestamps for unattended-device or device-security modules
audit/assessment reports listing policy non-compliance for screen lock/logoff
exceptions/waiver registry for devices or areas exempted from auto-lock
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous endpoint management logs showing enforced screen-lock configuration across devices and successful profile application timestamps; correlated authentication/session logs showing automatic lock events; training LMS records with completion timestamps; recurring audit reports showing low/no findings.

Weak: one-off screenshots or self-attestation spreadsheets; annual manual checks without configuration or log evidence; inconsistent or missing endpoint inventory making coverage calculations unreliable.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description requires policies, automatic screen lock features and restricted physical access — measures that prevent unauthorized use of unattended devices.

AST-10.differentiation_note: Unlike broader asset-management controls, this control specifically requires measurement of endpoint screen-lock configuration, user training on unattended devices, and incidents tied to unlocked workstations — i.e., technical enforcement plus human-behavior metrics rather than inventory or return-of-equipment metrics.

AST-11.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the IP asset register (percentage of IP assets identified and classified), the coverage and effectiveness of IP protection processes (percentage of projects with signed NDAs/IP-assignment/licensing clauses), training compliance (percentage of relevant employees completing IP training and assessment pass-rate), and the volume and remediation status of IP-related incidents (count of IP incidents and time-to-resolution).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AST-11 is primarily preventive (policy, identification, classification, and training) so percentage metrics (coverage, compliance, training completion) best show policy adherence; counts and trends supplement for incident volumes and audit frequency, and scores for training assessment effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of known IP assets recorded in the IP register with classification and assigned owner versus expected portfolio; 2) Percentage of active projects/contracts with executed NDAs/IP-assignment/licensing clauses; 3) Count of IP-related incidents and mean time to resolve per quarter; 4) Employee IP training completion rate and assessment pass rate within the required timeframe; 5) Number of IP audits performed and percent of audit findings remediated within policy timelines.
data_signals: IP asset register records (asset id, type: patent/trademark/copyright/trade secret, owner, classification, last review date)
repository of executed NDAs/licensing/assignment contracts with execution and countersignature dates
IP audit reports and associated remediation tickets (finding id, severity, open/closed status, remediation timestamps)
IP-related incident logs (incident id, date reported, incident type: infringement/theft/misuse, detection source, resolution date)
training/LMS records for IP awareness (enrollment date, completion date, quiz/assessment score, role targeting)
policy and procedure revision history (policy version, approval date, next review date)
IP portfolio valuation or registry summaries (portfolio counts by IP type, date of valuation)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a continuously maintained IP register with timestamps and owners, automated contract repository showing executed NDAs/licences, and LMS records with assessment scores and completion timestamps.
Strong: recurring IP audit cycles with documented findings and remediation tickets showing closure dates within policy timelines.
Weak: one-off/manual attestations that IP exists without timestamped records, ad-hoc training attendance without assessments, or narrative descriptions of protections without contract metadata.
Weak: annual-only penetration or legal reviews without regular audits or up-to-date contract inventories that demonstrate ongoing protection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description mandates developing/maintaining policy, conducting regular IP audits, training, and contracts (NDAs/licences) which are preventative measures to avoid IP loss or misuse.

AST-11.differentiation_note: This control focuses on legal/asset-management and personnel/process measures (policy, IP register, contracts, training, audits) rather than technical controls; measurement must emphasize inventory completeness, contract coverage, audit remediation, and training effectiveness rather than system configuration or vulnerability metrics.

BCD-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical information assets with successful, encrypted backups that meet defined RPO targets, the rate and count of successful backup jobs and restoration tests, and the mean time to recover (restore) against RTO goals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control demanding coverage and compliance with RPO/RTO (coverage % of assets backed up on schedule); counts and rates (backup jobs, restore tests) and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show operational reliability and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Report the percentage of critical assets that had at least one successful, integrity-verified backup within the required RPO window during the reporting period; the count and timestamps of backup jobs (success/failure) per asset; the number and outcomes of restoration tests and the measured restore time (RTO) per test. Include encryption status and backup storage location (on-site/off-site/cloud) and retention metadata so compliance with policy and RTO/RPO can be determined.
data_signals: backup job execution timestamps and durations
backup job result codes / success-failure flags per asset
last successful backup timestamp per critical asset
restoration test execution records with timestamps, results, and measured restore time
RPO / RTO mapping per asset (policy table)
backup retention metadata and storage location tags (on-site / off-site / cloud)
encryption status and key identifier for backup media
backup activity logs and error/failure log entries
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated backup job logs with success/failure flags, checksums or integrity verification, documented RPO/RTO mapping, and successful, repeatable restoration test records showing measured restore times.
Weak: ad-hoc manual attestations, single point-in-time backup snapshots without integrity verification, or backup records lacking asset mapping and RPO/RTO context.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates regular backups, defined RPO/RTO and protective measures (encryption, storage) which are preventive actions to avoid data loss.

BCD-7.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on backup coverage, encryption, and restoration-test evidence (RPO/RTO compliance), unlike sibling controls that might focus on incident response, DR plan activation, or assignment of responsibilities; its unique measurement need is asset-level backup success and restore-time evidence tied to RPO/RTO.

BCD-8.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and readiness of the information security continuity plan: percentage of critical information assets covered by a formal continuity plan, frequency and success rate of plan tests/exercises, mean time to restore (RTO) for critical systems during tests or incidents, and percentage of personnel with assigned continuity roles who completed required training.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily preventive (planning and coverage) so percentage metrics best show coverage and adherence (e.g., % of critical assets covered, % of staff trained). Counts and trends are useful secondary measures for exercise frequency, incident counts, and RTO trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percentage of critical information assets that have an associated, dated information security continuity plan or documented recovery procedure; (2) test execution rate and success rate (count of planned tests vs executed, and % of tests meeting acceptance criteria); (3) mean time to restore (RTO) observed in tests and real incidents per critical system; (4) percentage of assigned continuity-role personnel who completed required training and tabletop exercises within the last 12 months.
data_signals: asset inventory with criticality classification and mapping to continuity plan IDs
information security continuity plan documents with version and last-update timestamps
plan roles and responsibilities register (named owners and contact details)
plan test/exercise schedule and execution logs (dates, type: tabletop/full recovery, participants)
test outcomes and after-action reports (success/failure, issues, remediation actions)
restore/recovery time measurements per test or incident (timestamps: outage start, recovery complete)
incident records noting data loss during disruptive events and linkage to continuity plan
training attendance/completion records for continuity roles (course IDs, completion dates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated test execution logs with timestamps and measured RTOs are strong evidence (demonstrates repeatable, measured recovery capability).
Comprehensive asset-to-plan mapping and dated plan versions are strong evidence of coverage; undocumented or generic plans are weak.
After-action reports with tracked remediation items and closure evidence strengthen confidence; presence of plan documents alone is weak evidence.
Training completion records tied to role assignments increase evidence strength; anecdotal or unscheduled training is weaker.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes planning, developing a continuity plan, assigning roles, and preventive measures (e.g., 'development of a formal information security continuity plan', 'embed information security into BCM'), which are preventive activities.

BCD-8.differentiation_note: This control is focused on planning and coverage (asset-to-plan mapping, documented recovery strategies, training and test cadence) rather than detection or incident handling—measurements must show plan existence, asset coverage, test outcomes and measured RTOs rather than alert volumes or vulnerability counts.

BCD-9.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of the Business Continuity Plan (BCP) for information security by tracking: (1) percentage of critical systems with documented RTOs/RPOs and successful restore test results, (2) mean time to recover (MTTR) observed in tests/incidents versus RTO targets, and (3) percentage of staff who completed BCP/continuity training and number/frequency of validated drills and plan reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires proving coverage and adherence to defined targets (RTO/RPO, staff training, plan review frequency) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) directly show preventive control effectiveness; counts and trends supplement with drill frequency and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percent of critical information assets/systems that have documented RTO and RPO values and have passed a restore test within those targets; 2) MTTR measured in actual incidents and formal drills compared to RTO by asset; 3) Percentage of staff in scope who completed BCP/continuity training and the frequency of documented drills and plan reviews. Data must include timestamps and outcome of restores to allow SLA comparisons.
data_signals: BCP document version and last review timestamp
catalog of critical information assets with assigned RTO and RPO values
backup job execution logs and backup completion timestamps
restore test execution records with measured time-to-restore and success/failure status
mean time to recover (MTTR) from drills and real incidents by asset
training completion records for BCP (user, course, completion date)
test/drill schedules, execution logs, and after-action reports with identified gaps
incidents where RTO/RPO missed (count and root-cause) and remediation tracking
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated backup and restore test logs with timestamps and successful restore outcomes, repeated drill results showing MTTR consistently within RTO, signed/dated BCP with scheduled reviews and tracked remediation from after-action reports, and training records tied to role assignments.
Weak: single tabletop exercise without measurable restore times, undated or unsigned BCP documents, anecdotal statements of readiness, or one-off manual screenshots of a restore.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on establishing plans, RTOs/RPOs, backups and defined procedures to maintain/restore security posture — activities that prevent prolonged downtime and breaches.

BCD-9.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about planning, recovery objectives (RTO/RPO), backups, training and drill outcomes (recovery effectiveness), differentiating it from sibling controls that may focus on detection, access control, or configuration management; measurement must emphasize restore/test results and SLA compliance rather than only incident counts.

BCD-10.measurement_goal: Measure the cadence and coverage of continuity tests (tabletop, simulation, full recovery), the percentage of continuity plans reviewed on schedule, the percentage of staff with assigned continuity roles who completed role-specific training, and the service restoration times (RTO/RPO) achieved per test versus SLA targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes verification and testing (detective assurance): rates (e.g., test success rate, SLA compliance rate) directly show effectiveness, while percentages (training coverage, plan-review compliance), counts (number of exercises), and trend metrics (improvement over time) provide complementary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and type of continuity tests executed per period; per-test measured RTO and RPO for each impacted service; rate of tests that met SLA targets; percentage of staff listed in continuity roles who completed role-specific training; number and age of open remediation/action items from tests and audits; dates of last plan review and updates.
data_signals: continuity test execution logs with timestamps and test type (tabletop / simulation / full failover)
measured RTO and RPO values per test by service
service restoration time per test/incident (timestamps: outage start, restoration)
continuity plan review records and version timestamps
staff training completion records mapped to continuity roles (attendance, completion date)
after-action/post-test reports with documented remediation items and remediation timestamps
planned vs completed continuity tests per schedule (test schedule calendar)
communication/notification logs with timestamps during tests or incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated test execution logs with timestamps, measured RTO/RPO per service, signed after-action reports, and tracked remediation items with closure timestamps.
Strong: training records tied to role assignments and completion dates showing coverage of required personnel.
Weak: single ad-hoc exercise without supporting logs, unsigned or undated plan documents, anecdotal recollections of tests, or isolated post-incident notes without measurable RTO/RPO data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes regular review, testing and evaluation (tabletop exercises, simulations, audits) which are detective assurance activities that verify effectiveness rather than purely preventing or correcting.

BCD-10.differentiation_note: This control is focused on verifying and testing information security continuity (test execution, measured RTO/RPO, training completion and remediation tracking) rather than on creating plans, backup configuration, or incident handling process details; its unique measurement need is measurable test outcomes and SLA comparison.

BCD-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage uptime of critical information processing systems and the success rate and timeliness of failover/recovery activities; measure frequency and pass-rate of business continuity/disaster recovery tests and training completion for personnel assigned emergency procedures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control specifies uptime and coverage targets (best expressed as percentages, e.g., uptime % and BCP test pass %). Secondary metrics (rates, counts, trends) capture incident/ failover frequencies, MTTR, and change over time for remediation and testing cadence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Per-critical-system uptime percentage vs. SLA, failover/recovery success rate and timestamps (including automated failover events), mean time to recover (MTTR) for availability incidents, frequency and results of BCP/DR tests, existence and currency of geographically-dispersed site inventory, and personnel training completion dates for emergency procedures and assigned responsibilities.
data_signals: system uptime metrics by system/component (monitoring telemetry with timestamps)
SLA uptime targets and per-system SLA assignment
incident records with detection, failover activation, and recovery timestamps (for MTTR calculation)
automated failover/replication event logs and success/failure codes
backup power and UPS test logs (timestamps and results)
BCP/DR test schedules, test execution reports and pass/fail outcomes
RTO and RPO definitions per system and last-tested date
training completion records for emergency procedures and assigned continuity responsibilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous monitoring telemetry (uptime metrics and automated failover logs) and system-assigned SLAs are strong evidence.
Completed, dated BCP/DR test reports with remediation items closed are strong; an undated plan document or a one-off tabletop exercise without results is weak.
Automated failover event logs with success codes (strong) vs. manual anecdotal reports of 'failover occurred' (weak).
Training records with timestamps and attendee lists (strong) vs. undocumented verbal confirmations (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires implementing redundancy and continuity measures (e.g., backup power, failover, dispersed data centers) and maintaining BCPs to prevent downtime, so it is primarily preventive.

BCD-11.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about ensuring availability and resilience of processing facilities (uptime, failover success, BCP testing and training) rather than access control, logging, or project-level security — measurement must focus on uptime/DR metrics and test evidence rather than configuration or vulnerability counts.

CAP-2.measurement_goal: Demonstrate continuous monitoring and planning by measuring (1) resource utilization (CPU, memory, disk, network) coverage across all production assets, (2) capacity headroom percentage against defined thresholds and (3) forecast vs actual resource demand and frequency of capacity plan reviews to show resources meet current and anticipated demand.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and sufficiency: percentages (e.g., percent of resources below utilization thresholds or percent of assets covered by monitoring) directly show capacity headroom; trends and distributions support forecasting and identify peak patterns; comparisons validate forecast accuracy versus actuals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Timestamped utilization samples per resource (CPU %, memory %, disk free %, network throughput/latency) aggregated by asset and criticality; inventory of hardware/software with coverage flags; defined capacity thresholds and SLAs/policy thresholds; capacity planning artifacts (forecast outputs, assumptions, review date) and incident logs showing outages or degraded performance attributed to resource shortage.
data_signals: CPU utilization time-series per host/VM/container (timestamped)
memory utilization time-series per host/VM/container (timestamped)
disk free space percentage per volume (timestamped)
network throughput and latency metrics per interface/service (timestamped)
asset inventory with resource type, criticality tag, and monitoring coverage flag
capacity plan documents and forecast outputs (dates, forecast vs actual values)
monitoring tool uptime/heartbeat and metric collection coverage ratios
incident/outage records tagged with "operational impact" or resource shortage root cause
regular KPI reports showing peak usage times and baseline measurements

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly labels it a "preventive control" and focuses on ensuring sufficient resources and proactive forecasting.

CAP-2.differentiation_note: This control is specifically focused on resource capacity forecasting, continuous utilization telemetry and planning artifacts (inventory + forecasts) rather than incident detection or configuration hardening; measurement must combine time-series utilization, asset coverage, and forecast-vs-actual comparisons.

CFG-2.measurement_goal: Measure RBAC coverage and enforcement: percentage of user accounts assigned to documented roles with least-privilege permissions, percentage of high-sensitivity assets mapped to access policies, time-to-revoke access after role changes, and counts of unauthorized access attempts detected per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control verifying coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of accounts with least-privilege, percent of assets classified/mapped). Counts and rates (unauthorized attempts, revocation events) are useful secondary signals to show enforcement and responsiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percent of active user accounts assigned to a documented RBAC role and evaluated for least-privilege; (2) mean and 95th-percentile time from role-change or termination event to access revocation; (3) percent of information assets with sensitivity classification mapped to access policies; (4) count/rate of unauthorized access attempts and failed authentications (MFA failures) per period.
data_signals: user role assignment records (RBAC) with assignment and last-reviewed timestamps
access entitlement lists per account (permission sets) and privilege level labels
access request/approval/revocation workflow logs with timestamps and approver IDs
authentication logs including MFA enabled flag, success/failure events and timestamps
access logs showing unauthorized access attempts and failure reasons with source IP
information asset inventory with sensitivity classification and mapping to access policies
access rights review/certification records and reviewer attestations with review cadence
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated IAM system logs (role assignments, entitlement exports, revocation timestamps) and regular attestation records are strong evidence.
Continuous authentication and access logs showing MFA enforcement and failed/successful attempts strengthen claims of enforcement.
Manual spreadsheets, one-off screenshots, or annual-only reviews are weak evidence because they do not demonstrate ongoing enforcement or timely revocation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control is described explicitly as a "preventive control" that "limits access to sensitive information" via RBAC, MFA, and formal granting/revoking processes.

CFG-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on RBAC coverage, access lifecycle (grant/modify/revoke) and asset classification mapping — measurement must emphasize entitlement lists, revocation latency, and access-review cadence rather than purely detection or remediation metrics.

CHG-4.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of the formal change request process: percentage of changes with documented assessment and formal approval (including CAB where required), mean time-to-approval by change type, count and percentage of changes that resulted in incidents, and rollback success rate for implemented changes.

data_signals: change request records with unique ID, requester, timestamps (requested, approved, implemented)
approval records (approver ID, approval timestamp, CAB decision/minutes)

change log entries and required-field completeness flags
incident tickets linked to change request ID and incident timestamps
rollback plan existence flag and rollback execution records (timestamp, success/failure)
change classification fields (standard/major/emergency) and SLA/time-to-approve values
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated change management system logs that link change request → approval → implementation with timestamps, CAB minutes stored, and automated linkage to incident/ticketing system; proves continuous, auditable coverage.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc email approvals without standardized fields or without linkage to implementation and incident records; provides only point-in-time or partial evidence.
Strong: documented rollback execution records showing successful reversion for failed changes; Weak: absence of rollback records or only anecdotal notes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls it a 'preventive control' and emphasizes assessing, approving and documenting changes to prevent unauthorized or poorly executed changes.

CHG-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the formal request/approval/documentation lifecycle (CAB, risk assessment, rollback, change log completeness) rather than implementation-only metrics or post-deployment monitoring, so measurements must tie request→approval→implementation→incident linkage.

CHG-5.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of changes that followed the formal lifecycle (submitted, assessed, authorized by CAB, tested, documented, communicated) and the success rate of deployments (successful vs. rollback) plus time-to-implement against policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage and adherence rates (e.g., % of changes approved/tested/completed successfully). Counts (change volume), rates (rollback rate), and trends (improvement over time) are useful secondary measures to show operational behavior and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track each change ticket from submission through CAB approval, test validation, deployment, and post-deployment status; compute percent of changes with recorded approvals and tests, count of rollbacks, and median time from approval to deployment. Include change metadata (owner, type, emergency flag) to filter policy-required vs emergency changes.
data_signals: change request submission timestamps and requestor IDs
CAB approval records (approver IDs, decision, timestamp) linked to change ticket IDs
test/validation artifacts or test result fields associated with change tickets
deployment/execution timestamps and target-system identifiers
change outcome status (successful / failed / rolled back) and rollback event records
change implementation duration (approval-to-deployment and submission-to-closure)
change classification (normal/emergency/standard) and reason fields
change log entries and stakeholder notification records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated change tickets with immutable CAB approval timestamps, attached test artifacts, deployment logs, and recorded rollback events covering 100% of production changes.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off PDF approvals, missing test evidence, ad-hoc undocumented emergency changes, or point-in-time snapshots without historical trend data.

affinity_keywords: successful versus those that required rollback

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes planning, assessment, authorization (CAB), testing and documentation steps intended to prevent unauthorized or unsafe changes.

CHG-5.differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on measuring end-to-end change lifecycle adherence (approvals, test evidence, rollbacks, and timing) rather than related controls that might focus purely on post-change incident detection or configuration baselines.

CHG-6.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of platform change events that have a completed technical review for each affected application, the count and severity of issues identified during those reviews, and the time-to-remediation and post-change failure rate per change window.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires demonstrating coverage (e.g., percentage of changes reviewed) as the primary proof; supporting counts (vulnerabilities found), rates (remediation and failure rates) and trends over time show effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each operating platform change event, record the set of affected applications, a timestamped technical review record (checklist completion status, reviewer identity), vulnerabilities or issues identified with severity, remediation ticket IDs and timestamps for fix/closure, and any post-change incidents/failures within a defined observation window.
data_signals: platform change event ID and timestamp
list of affected application IDs/names per change
technical review checklist completion status and reviewer(s)
vulnerability scan results or static analysis findings associated with the review (issue ID, severity, CVE if applicable)
remediation ticket ID, creation and closure timestamps, assigned owner
post-change incident/failure records and timestamps (errors, outages) within X days
number of applications reviewed vs. applications affected per change
documentation artifacts linked to the review (report PDFs, signed findings)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped review records and linked vulnerability scan outputs per change plus remediation tickets with closure timestamps and evidence of testing after fix
Strong: checklist items signed by named reviewers (developers, security, sysadmins) and stored in change management system
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals or undocumented verbal sign-offs without timestamps or linkage to change/event IDs
Weak: one-off manual test reports without remediation tickets or post-change incident correlation

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control's purpose is to ensure changes do not adversely affect applications ('ensure that any modifications ... do not adversely affect'), which is primarily preventive by establishing reviews and checklists before/after changes.

CHG-6.differentiation_note: Unlike general change governance controls, this control specifically requires application-level technical reviews tied to each platform change and thus needs per-change coverage, review checklist completion, vulnerability findings, and remediation timelines rather than only high-level change approval records.

CPL-3.measurement_goal: Measure audit program coverage and effectiveness by counting scheduled vs. completed internal and external ISMS audits, measuring the percentage of in-scope systems/processes audited, and tracking remediation: percent of findings remediated within SLA, age and severity distribution of open findings, and presence of complete audit reports/audit trail.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Audits and findings are best evidenced by counts (number of audits performed, number of findings) while remediation effectiveness requires percentages (remediated within timeframe) and trends to show improvement over time; counts align with the control's emphasis on 'number of audits' and 'document findings'.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Produce a dataset linking scheduled audit engagements to executed audit records (planned date, execution date, auditor), mapping each audit to an explicit scope of systems/processes; for each audit finding capture severity, finding creation timestamp, remediation ticket ID, remediation owner, remediation open and close timestamps, and the SLA target for that severity so remediation timeliness (percent remediated within SLA) and open-finding age by severity can be computed.
data_signals: audit schedule records: planned audit date, audit type (internal/external), assigned auditor
audit execution logs: actual execution date, scope/components covered, audit report ID
audit reports: finding IDs, finding descriptions, finding severity/classification
remediation tickets: ticket ID, assigned owner, created timestamp, closed timestamp, remediation status
SLA / remediation timeframe by finding severity or finding type
audit trail artifacts: report sign-offs, distribution list, evidence attachments
auditor qualifications/engagement letters and independence documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated audit schedule matched to executed audit records and machine-readable audit reports with finding IDs linked to remediation tickets and closure timestamps
Strong: documented SLA targets by severity and consistent remediation timestamps showing closure within SLA
Weak: one-off or manual PDF reports without machine-linkable finding IDs or missing remediation ticket references
Weak: anecdotal sign-off emails or unsigned reports that do not show an audit trail or auditor qualifications

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on evaluating effectiveness, identifying vulnerabilities and verifying controls ('systematically evaluate', 'identify vulnerabilities', 'assess risks', 'verify') which are detective activities.

CPL-3.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of an audit program (schedule, scope, auditor qualifications) plus remediation tracking (findings → tickets → SLA closure), distinguishing it from sibling controls that might focus only on technical detection or policy definition; measurement must link audits to remediation artifacts rather than only counting reports.

CPL-4.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the centralized inventory of applicable laws, regulations and contract obligations (coverage %), the count of mappings from each requirement to organizational policy/process owners, and the frequency and timeliness of documented reviews/updates against a defined review cadence.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive completeness/coverage control so coverage percentage (percent of applicable requirements inventoried and mapped) is the most meaningful; counts of requirements and trending of updates provide operational detail and show maintenance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of applicable legal/regulatory/contractual requirements that are (a) recorded in the central repository, (b) mapped to a responsible policy or process, and (c) reviewed/updated within the required review period; plus counts of new/changed requirements and number overdue for mapping or review.
data_signals: centralized requirements inventory records (requirement ID, citation, jurisdiction/contract reference)
mapping table linking requirement IDs to policy IDs and process owners
inventory creation and last-reviewed timestamps
change-monitoring alerts/legislation watch logs with detected changes and timestamps
policy update/change ticket records referencing requirement IDs
number of requirements with no assigned owner or mapping (unmapped_count)
scheduled review cadence records and executed review logs (attestation records)
overdue requirement/mapping counts and SLA breach records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated centralized inventory with timestamps, requirement-to-policy mapping, and documented review attestations showing regular updates
Strong: integration with legislation-change feeds or vendor regulatory watch and tracked change tickets that reference requirement IDs
Weak: single point-in-time spreadsheets or ad-hoc documents without timestamps/owners or without mapping to policies
Weak: undocumented manual statements from staff without repository records or evidence of scheduled reviews

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires identification, documentation and proactive maintenance of legal/contractual requirements (phrases: 'identify', 'documented', 'regularly reviewed'), which prevents compliance gaps.

CPL-4.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires an auditable inventory and mapping of legal/regulatory/contractual obligations and change-monitoring, unlike sibling operational controls which focus on technical controls or incident handling.

CPL-5.measurement_goal: Measure completion and timeliness of scheduled independent ISMS reviews, the count and severity distribution of findings per review, and the percentage and time-to-complete of corrective actions implemented in response to those findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Independent reviews produce discrete events and findings (best measured as counts of reviews and findings). Percentages (e.g., % of recommendations implemented) and trends over time are useful to show remediation effectiveness and improving ISMS posture as required by the control.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of independent reviews executed in the reporting period (with review date, scope, and reviewer identity/type); count and severity classification of findings per review; remediation tickets mapped to each finding with open and close timestamps to calculate time-to-remediate; percentage of findings with implemented corrective actions within the organization's required timeframe.
data_signals: independent review execution records (review date, scope, reviewer identity/type: internal audit or external consultant)
final review reports containing findings and recommendations
finding severity or category tags (e.g., compliance, operational, technical)
corrective action/remediation tickets with created and closed timestamps and owner
evidence of reviewer independence (engagement letter, conflict-of-interest attestation)
schedule/calendar of periodic assessments (e.g., annual review schedule) and completion status
status of employee awareness training assessments when reviewed
policy-to-findings mapping (which policy/objective each finding references)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Signed independent review reports plus linked remediation tickets showing implemented changes and close timestamps (demonstrates both detection and remediation).
Strong: Documentation of reviewer independence (engagement letters) and a maintained schedule showing periodic completion (at least annually).
Weak: Single annual summary memo without individual findings, missing remediation ticket linkage, or unsigned/undated reports.
Weak: Oral assertions or ad-hoc meeting notes without documented findings, timestamps, or proof of independence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes conducting reviews to identify vulnerabilities and assess compliance, which is primarily a detective activity ('identify vulnerabilities', 'evaluate the ISMS').

CPL-5.differentiation_note: This control is governance-focused and requires measuring audit execution, reviewer independence, findings and remediation tracking — unlike technical controls it emphasizes reviewer scope, report evidence and corrective-action linkage rather than configuration or detection telemetry.

CPL-6.measurement_goal: Measure policy and ISMS adherence by reporting the percentage of employees who completed required security training on schedule, the percentage of policies reviewed/updated within the defined review cycle, ISMS scope coverage (assets/processes included vs. required), and the count and closure rate of internal-audit nonconformities and policy violations over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive ISMS/policy control: auditors expect coverage and adherence percentages (e.g., training completion %, policy coverage %) as primary evidence; counts (policy violations, nonconformities), rates (remediation closure rate) and compliance scores supplement coverage metrics.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Produce queries/reports that yield: (a) percent of employees (by role/team) who completed required security training within required timeframe; (b) percent of active security policies with a documented owner and a 'last reviewed' date within the policy review cycle; (c) ISMS scope coverage as count of required assets/processes included vs. inventory; (d) counts and mean time-to-close for internal-audit nonconformities and policy-violation incidents.
data_signals: training completion records with employee ID, role, course ID, completion timestamp
policy repository metadata: policy ID, owner, version, publish_date, last_review_date
ISMS scope statement and asset/process inventory with inclusion flags
internal audit reports listing nonconformity IDs, severity, open_date, close_date, responsible_owner
corrective action / ticketing records with status and resolution timestamps
policy violation incident logs with incident ID, timestamps, impacted data/asset, remediation status
management review minutes or audit evidence of policy updates and approval

Training LMS records with timestamps and attestations (strong) vs. aggregated emailed lists without timestamps or role mapping (weak).
Internal audit reports with tracked nonconformity tickets and closure dates (strong) vs. audit observations recorded only in meeting minutes without remediation tracking (weak).
Up-to-date ISMS scope and asset inventory stored in configuration/CMDB (strong) vs. ad-hoc lists or undocumented scope (weak).

affinity_keywords: Compliance with security policies and standards

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' focused on ensuring adherence to policies and ISMS processes (e.g., training, documented roles, regular reviews).

CPL-6.differentiation_note: This control is ISMS/policy-level and focuses on policy lifecycle, training completion, ISMS scope and audit nonconformity closure — unlike technical siblings it requires measuring document metadata, role assignments and organizational coverage rather than technical telemetry.

CPL-7.measurement_goal: Measure review coverage and effectiveness of technical compliance activities: percentage of technical assets included in scheduled reviews, count of compliance gaps found by category, and remediation velocity (time-to-remediate) against policy-defined targets per review cycle.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute asset-level review coverage (number of assets reviewed / total assets in the technical asset inventory) per review cycle; count non-compliance findings by category (configurations, access controls, updates), and measure time from finding creation to remediation closure and percent remediated within policy thresholds.
data_signals: technical asset inventory (asset ID, owner, classification, last-reviewed timestamp)
review schedule and execution timestamps (scheduled date, executed date, reviewer)
review reports / findings records (finding ID, category: configuration|access_control|update, severity, description)
remediation tickets (creation timestamp, assigned owner, remediation completion timestamp, status)
configuration audit results (pre/post remediation configuration states)
access control audit logs referenced by review (changes, exceptions, approvals)
patch/update status per system (patch level, last update timestamp)
evidence of manual assessment checklists and automated tool scan reports (tool name, scan timestamps, results)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated scan reports plus linked remediation tickets and timestamps showing closure within policy thresholds across consecutive review cycles.
Strong: complete technical asset inventory with last-reviewed timestamps and owner attribution proving full coverage.
Weak: one-off manual review reports without timestamps or unresolved findings, or missing linkage between findings and remediation tickets.
Weak: incomplete asset inventory or undocumented manual notes that cannot be traced to remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing schedules, ensuring alignment with standards, and maintaining inventories to prevent non-compliance ('establish a schedule', 'ensure adherence to regulatory requirements'), indicating a primarily preventive control.

CPL-7.differentiation_note: This control focuses on scheduled, cross-functional technical compliance reviews (coverage, documented findings, and remediation tracking across configurations, access controls, and updates), not merely vulnerability scanning or incident response metrics; it requires asset-level coverage and evidence of policy-driven review cadence.

CPL-8.measurement_goal: Measure audit program coverage and execution (number of audits performed vs planned, scope coverage), count and severity distribution of audit findings, and the percentage and timeliness of corrective actions closed against defined remediation SLAs so an auditor can verify ISMS effectiveness and issue remediation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Counts (audits performed, findings opened/closed) directly demonstrate detective activity; percentages show closure/compliance rates and SLA adherence; rates and trends show remediation velocity and program improvement over time, matching the control's emphasis on number of audits, percent resolved, and time to address non-conformities.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of internal audits executed per audit period and per ISMS domain; mapping of audits completed vs planned (coverage matrix); count and severity of non-conformities/findings; for each finding record: open timestamp, assigned owner, remediation SLA, close timestamp and evidence of closure; auditor qualification/independence records and documented audit scope/objectives.
data_signals: audit schedule (planned audits) and execution timestamps (audit start/end dates)
audit scope and objectives documents (audit charters)
internal audit reports with findings listed and severity/classification
corrective action / non-conformity records with owner, open timestamp, remediation SLA, close timestamp, and evidence attachments
audit checklist versions mapped to ISO 27001:2013 clauses
auditor qualification records and independence declarations
management review minutes or communications showing receipt of audit results
coverage matrix: audits completed vs planned by ISMS domain/component

Moderate: documented audit reports and evidence of closure stored in a document repository but lacking structured timestamps for automated measurement.
Weak: isolated PDF audit reports or one-off summaries without traceable corrective action records or auditor qualification evidence (cannot demonstrate timely remediation or consistent coverage).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes evaluating ISMS effectiveness, assessing compliance, and identifying non-conformities (detective activities) and documenting findings.

CPL-8.differentiation_note: This control focuses on program-level audit coverage, auditor qualifications, and corrective-action lifecycle metrics (counts, closure rates, MTTR), unlike sibling controls that would measure technical control telemetry or continuous monitoring — it therefore requires audit schedules, reports, and corrective-action records rather than system logs alone.

CPL-9.measurement_goal: Measure the count and rate of reported nonconformities, the time-to-resolution and percent completed within defined timelines, and the effectiveness/recurrence rate of corrective actions (including verification that root causes were addressed).

Counts (number of nonconformities), percentages (closed within timelines) and trends (recurrence over time) complement the rate primary measure to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of nonconformity records by severity and business unit; for each record capture report creation timestamp, assigned owner/role, root-cause analysis completion timestamp, corrective action start and completion timestamps, verification/closure evidence and a recurrence flag linking to prior similar records. data_signals: nonconformity report ID (ticket/reference)
report creation timestamp and reporter identity
assigned owner / roles and responsibilities field
root cause analysis completed flag and timestamp
corrective action start and completion timestamps
verification/closure evidence (signed-off by verifier) and verification timestamp
linkage to internal audit finding IDs or risk assessment references
recurrence_count or recurrence flag for same issue across periods
stakeholder feedback or corrective-action satisfaction survey results

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text explicitly describes identifying deviations and implementing corrective actions and preventing recurrence, which are corrective activities (root cause analysis, timelines, corrective measures).

CPL-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses on measuring corrective action lifecycle and effectiveness (time-to-resolve, recurrence) rather than detecting incidents or enforcing preventive configurations; measurements must capture closure verification and linkage to audits/risks.

CRY-6.measurement_goal: Measure policy coverage and operational enforcement: percentage of systems and data classes protected by approved cryptographic algorithms/protocols, count and status of cryptographic keys tracked in the key management system (creation/rotation/revocation), frequency of policy reviews, and percentage of relevant staff completing cryptography training on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive, policy/governance control where coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., % systems using approved crypto, % staff trained) best show effectiveness; counts (keys, non-approved configs) and trends over time support operational monitoring and periodic comparison to policy thresholds.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of production systems and datasets that use approved cryptographic algorithms/protocols; count of managed keys in KMS with lifecycle metadata (owner, creation, rotation, revocation timestamps); frequency and date of formal policy reviews; number and percent of staff in scope who completed cryptography training; count of incidents or breaches attributed to cryptographic failures.
data_signals: asset inventory records with cryptographic algorithm/protocol field (algorithm name, protocol version)
key management system (KMS) logs: key id, owner, creation/rotation/revocation timestamps, algorithm, key status
configuration management / IaC scan results indicating crypto library versions and cipher suites in use
policy document version history and policy review timestamps
training records: enrollments, completions, completion timestamps for cryptography training
incident/ breach logs citing 'encryption' or 'cryptographic failure' as cause
scan/list of systems using non-approved cryptographic algorithms or deprecated protocols
patch/update records for cryptographic libraries and applied date

Strong: policy versioning with dated reviews and documented approved algorithm list matched to deployed configs.
Weak: one-off spreadsheets or screenshots asserting compliance without machine-verifiable logs.
Weak: annual manual attestations with no linkage to KMS or configuration data, or incidents present without root-cause linking to cryptography controls.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes policy, approved algorithms and key management procedures — governance actions intended to prevent misuse or improper encryption.

key rotation event records (timestamp, key ID, actor, rotation reason)
key destruction/retirement records with timestamps and evidence
HSM usage logs and inventory mapping (key ID -> HSM serial/slot)
key access request and approval logs (requester, approver, justification, timestamp)
key storage location attributes (software keystore vs. HSM vs. cloud KMS)
cryptographic metadata (algorithm, key length, key purpose flags) per key ID
audit trail entries for key access and administrative actions

secure_email_gateway logs (DLP blocks, phishing detections, attachment blocks with timestamps and recipient/source)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on policy, encryption requirements, secure gateways and configuration to prevent unauthorized access and breaches (e.g., "encryption requirements", "prevent unauthorized access"), indicating primarily preventive controls.

CRY-9.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of public-facing application services that enforce strong encryption (TLS/SSL) and strong authentication, track the count and severity of vulnerabilities and incidents originating from public networks, and measure mean time to detect and remediate vulnerabilities/incidents against policy/SLA targets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on implementing protective measures (encryption, gateways, authentication, policies) which are preventive controls, while also noting monitoring and assessments as supporting activities.

CRY-10.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of application service transactions protected by organization-approved encryption (e.g., TLS with approved cipher suites and valid certificates), the percentage of transaction endpoints enforcing authentication/authorization, the count/rate of unauthorized transaction attempts or transaction-related security incidents, and remediation velocity for vulnerabilities affecting transaction protection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes preventing unauthorized access and data manipulation via encryption (TLS), access controls, secure coding and WAFs — all preventive measures (though it also references assessments and monitoring).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls it a 'preventive security measure' focused on establishing policy, permitted algorithms and key management to prevent unauthorized access.

DCH-12.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of applied classification across information assets (percentage of assets/documents with an approved classification label), the count and rate of misclassification incidents and their remediation timeliness, and employee training/adherence metrics (training completion % and frequency of classification reviews) to demonstrate the classification policy is implemented and effective.

data_signals: classification_policy_document (version, effective_date)
asset/document inventory with assigned classification label and classification_timestamp

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates classification and labelling to ensure appropriate handling and prevent unauthorized access (policy, labels visible, automated tools), which is preventive in nature.

DCH-14.measurement_goal: Measure inventory coverage and classification completeness for all organizational assets (information, hardware, software), the percentage of assets with assigned owners and labels, the frequency of inventory updates, and the completion rate of asset-handling training and documented disposal actions to demonstrate adherence to asset handling policies.

DCH-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints and removable media assets that are inventoried and encrypted, the coverage rate of endpoint controls that block or log unauthorized removable devices, and counts of removable-media-related incidents and policy violations per period to demonstrate enforcement and reduction of data exposure risk.

Strong: inventory records listing device serials/UUIDs and encryption state with timestamps and custodianship fields.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policies and procedures to ensure secure irreversible destruction and prevention of unauthorized access (e.g., 'ensure the secure and irreversible destruction', 'media disposal policy'), which are preventive controls.

DCH-17.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of physical media transfers that follow policy-required controls (logged with chain-of-custody, encrypted, packaged with tamper-evident materials, and transported by approved couriers) and the count and rate of incidents (loss, theft, tampering, or unauthorized access) related to physical media per reporting period.

chain_of_custody records / handover signatures and handler IDs
tracking system telemetry (location timestamps, delivery confirmations)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on establishing procedures, encryption, packaging, approved couriers and access limits to prevent loss or unauthorized access, indicating a preventive control.

DCH-18.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information transfers that use approved secure transfer methods (encryption in transit and approved protocols), count and rate of transmission-related security incidents, and the percentage of staff and third-parties compliant with data-transfer roles, responsibilities, and training requirements.

END-2.measurement_goal: Measure endpoint and network coverage of anti-malware controls (percentage of systems with up-to-date AV/anti-malware and application whitelisting), detection activity (count of malware detections and IDS-correlated alerts), and response effectiveness (mean time to remediate infected hosts and percent of incidents remediated within SLA).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Although the description covers detect and respond, the primary emphasis is on deploying and keeping anti-malware, patch management, whitelisting and inventory (preventive measures) — hence preventive with moderate confidence.

END-2.differentiation_note: This control uniquely spans prevention, detection and response for malware across endpoints, servers and network devices and therefore requires combined coverage (percentage) metrics plus detection counts and remediation timing, whereas sibling controls (if present) might focus only on detection or patching alone.

END-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of operational systems that have only approved software installed, the count and rate of unauthorized software installation events detected, and the remediation/approval timelines for detected unauthorized installs against policy SLAs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/compliance measurement (percentage of endpoints with only approved software). Counts and rates (unauthorized installs, remediation events) are useful secondary metrics to show event volume and remediation velocity; trends show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of in-scope endpoints whose installed software inventory matches the approved-software inventory; count and rate of unauthorized software installation events detected (by whitelist/EDR) per time period; time from detection to remediation or formal approval; frequency and results of policy compliance audits. Include training completion rates for staff responsible for installations.
data_signals: approved software inventory records (canonical list of allowed applications)
per-endpoint installed-software scan results (timestamped)
application whitelisting / enforcement logs (allow/deny events with timestamps)
EDR/AV alerts for unauthorized installations or suspicious installers
software installation approval requests and approval/rejection timestamps
audit reports and audit findings for software installation policy compliance
incident reports tied to software vulnerabilities or unauthorized installs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, centralized whitelisting/enforcement logs correlated with endpoint inventory showing 100% match for in-scope systems; timestamped approval records and automated remediation workflows.
Weak: point-in-time manual inventories, one-off screenshots, or annual attestations that do not show active enforcement or detection of new unauthorized installs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description mandates preventing unauthorized/ insecure software via policy, approval process, whitelisting and inventory — all preventive measures.

END-3.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on authorization, whitelisting and policy enforcement for software installations on operational systems (prevention/coverage), rather than vulnerability discovery or incident response which measure detection/remediation of exploitable weaknesses.
