END-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user endpoints and accounts subject to enforced software-installation rules, the count and rate of blocked or unauthorized installation attempts, and the number and status of approved exceptions to the installation policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of endpoints with installation restrictions enforced). Supplementary counts of blocked installs and rates of attempts show operational effectiveness and exception activity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percent of managed endpoints with installation restrictions enabled (MDM/endpoint policy active), (2) count and rate of blocked/unauthorized software installation events per period, (3) number of user accounts with local/software install privileges, and (4) number and age/status of approved installation exceptions or change requests.
data_signals: software installation policy document (established/implemented) and version
MDM/endpoint configuration enforcement status per endpoint
application installer execution logs (installer binaries executed, timestamps, user account)
blocked installation events/alerts (policy enforcement logs)
user role/privilege assignments (accounts with install/local admin rights)
approved exception or change request records for software installation (ticket id, approver, expiry)
inventory of allowed/approved software vs newly installed software
endpoint compliance scan results showing installation restriction settings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated enforcement logs showing blocked installs and endpoint config scans indicating installation restrictions enabled across endpoints
Moderate: recent MDM/EDR configuration reports plus change/exception ticket records
Weak: policy document only or one-time manual attestations without system-enforced evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text requires that 'Rules ... shall be established and implemented', indicating preventive controls to stop unauthorized software installation.

END-4.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about restricting user-driven software installation and enforcing that policy on endpoints, unlike siblings that might address vulnerability scanning, patching, or general change approval — measurement must focus on endpoint enforcement, install events, and exception handling.

GOV-14.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the information security policy set (percent of required policies defined, approved and published), the proportion of policies with assigned owners/responsibilities, and the rate of policy communication/acknowledgement to personnel and inclusion of security requirements in project plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about policy coverage and communication; percentage metrics (coverage, percent current/approved, percent acknowledged) directly show compliance with policy lifecycle requirements, while counts and rates (number of unassigned policies, acknowledgement rate) and a policy maturity score provide complementary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of required information security policies that are: (a) created and stored in the policy repository, (b) assigned an owner, (c) approved with an approval date, (d) published/distributed, and (e) acknowledged by personnel. Also measure percent of active project plans that include an information security section or risk treatment entry and count of policies overdue for review against the mandated review cycle.
data_signals: policy repository records: policy_id, owner, approval_date, last_review_date, status (draft/approved/published)
publication/distribution logs or intranet publication timestamps for each policy
employee policy acknowledgement receipts or signed acknowledgement timestamps
policy review schedule and meeting minutes showing review outcomes
project plan metadata or templates with a security section flag and project-level risk treatment entries
RACI / responsibility assignment records showing allocated information security roles
overdue-policy report: policies past review_date or without approval within required timeframe
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated policy-management metadata with approval signatures, publication timestamps, and employee acknowledgement records; project plans with explicit security sections recorded in project management system.
Weak: Ad-hoc PDF copies on shared drives without approval metadata, or one-off attestations from staff; absence of RACI or project-plan evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The associated risks describe failures to define/review/approve/publish/communicate policies and to assign responsibilities — activities that prevent security lapses, so the control is preventive.

GOV-14.differentiation_note: This control focuses on policy lifecycle, assignment of responsibilities and communication into projects and personnel (policy coverage and acknowledgement), not on technical controls or operational incident response; measurement must target repository metadata, owner assignments and project-plan inclusion rather than technical telemetry.

GOV-15.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of information security policy reviews and approvals (policy-level last_review_date and approver presence), the publication/distribution status of each policy, and the proportion of personnel or project plans that have acknowledged or incorporated those policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventative governance control where auditors expect coverage and timeliness percentages (e.g., % policies reviewed on schedule, % personnel acknowledged). Counts and trends supplement to show volume and historical improvement; comparisons help show against policy thresholds or previous periods.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of active information security policies and the number with documented review events within the required review period; percentage of policies with recorded approver and published_date; count and percentage of employees or role groups who have acknowledged each policy; count of projects referencing/including security policy requirements in project plans.
data_signals: policy inventory with policy_id, owner, current_version, published_date, last_review_date
policy approval records (approver_id, approval_date, approval_signature or ticket)
publication/distribution logs (published_date, distribution_channel, document URL)
employee acknowledgement/attestation records per policy (user_id, timestamp, version acknowledged)
policy-to-project mapping or project plan artifacts indicating referenced security policies
policy review schedule and overdue review indicators (scheduled_review_date, review_status)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy repository records showing explicit approval signatures, published_date, version history, and machine-read acknowledgements tied to user IDs.
Strong: project plan artifacts referencing policy IDs and timestamps showing incorporation of policy requirements.
Weak: informal email threads or undated PDFs without approver metadata; single one-off attestations without linkage to policy version or review date.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on reviewing, approving, publishing and communicating policies — actions that prevent personnel from being uninformed, characteristic of a preventive governance control.

GOV-15.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on policy lifecycle (review/approval/publication/communication) and evidence of personnel or project incorporation, unlike operational controls that measure technical enforcement or incident response metrics.

GOV-16.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the register of relevant authorities and interest‑group contacts (percentage of required contacts with verified, up-to-date details) and count of formal communications/agreements with those contacts; additionally measure evidence that authorities are notified within the organisation's defined notification timeframe when breaches occur.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on maintaining coverage and currency of contacts, so percentage metrics (coverage/verification rate) best show compliance; counts (number of contacts, agreements, notifications) and trends (verification cadence over time) provide complementary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is: (1) percentage of required external authority and interest-group contacts whose contact information has been verified within the organisation's defined verification period (e.g., last 12 months); (2) count of required contacts vs. recorded contacts (full coverage); and (3) count and timestamps of authority notifications for incident events compared to the policy-defined notification window.
data_signals: official contact register / directory entries for relevant authorities and interest groups (name, role, contact details)
last verified / last contacted timestamp per contact
evidence of formal agreements or published contact points (MOU, published contact page, membership records)
records of communications with authorities (emails, submission receipts) with timestamps
incident notification logs showing authority notified and notification timestamp
policy/procedure document specifying verification frequency and notification timeframe
audit/review records showing periodic review and approval of contact list
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated contact registry with per-contact last-verified timestamps and routine review/audit logs showing periodic verification; documented MOUs or published contact endpoints; incident logs proving notifications occurred within the policy timeframe.
Weak: a single static list or one-off document without verification timestamps, or manual notes with no evidence of review or incident notifications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control states contacts 'shall be maintained', indicating an ongoing/preventive requirement to ensure preparedness before incidents occur.

GOV-16.differentiation_note: Unlike operational incident-handling controls, this control specifically requires measurement of the existence, currency, and verification cadence of external contact information (contact register health) rather than detection or remediation metrics.

GOV-17.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the registered contacts with special interest groups (percent of required contacts verified within policy timeframe) and demonstrate active engagement (count of communications/meetings and incidents escalated to those contacts and average response time) to show preparedness for breach response and threat awareness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about maintaining coverage and currency of required contacts, so percentage coverage (current vs required) is most appropriate; counts of contacts and rates/trends of engagement verify activity and responsiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required special interest group contacts that have complete contact details and a 'last verified' date within the organization-defined review period; count of documented communications/meetings with those groups per period; count of incidents that escalated to those contacts and average response time from contact first attempted to response received; presence of formal agreements/MOUs or subscription confirmations where applicable.
data_signals: special_interest_group contact registry entries (organization, role, contact method, primary contact, alternate contact, last_verified_date)
membership/MOU/subscription documents or confirmations with timestamps
communication records and meeting minutes with these groups (timestamps, attendees, topics)
incident escalation logs referencing external contact used (incident_id, contact_attempt_timestamp, first_response_timestamp)
policy or procedure records specifying required groups and review frequency (document id, last_review_date)
verification/audit records showing periodic contact verification actions and outcomes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated contact registry with last_verified_date within policy timeframe, signed MOUs/membership records, recurring meeting minutes, and incident logs showing successful escalation and measured response times.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots of a contact list, ad-hoc email chains without verification dates, or undocumented verbal confirmations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires maintaining contacts and relationships to prevent delays and lack of threat awareness, which proactively reduces risk rather than detecting or correcting incidents.

GOV-17.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of external contact registry completeness, verification cadence, and engagement/response metrics with special interest groups, rather than internal policy publication or role allocation metrics.

GOV-18.measurement_goal: Measure existence and currency of the mobile device policy (approved version and last review), the percent of personnel who have received and acknowledged the policy, the percent of active projects that include mobile-device security considerations, and the count/status of mobile-device risks recorded and treated in the risk register.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (policy existence, distribution and acknowledgement) so percentage metrics (coverage/acknowledgement/compliance rates) best show coverage; counts track risk items and projects, trends show review cadence and remediation progress, and a score can summarize policy lifecycle health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Existence and metadata of the published mobile device policy: version id, approval timestamp, and last review timestamp. 2) Distribution and acknowledgement: number of personnel who have acknowledged the policy vs. total employee headcount, with acknowledgement timestamps and user IDs. 3) Project coverage: count and percentage of active project plans containing an information-security/mobile-device section. 4) Mobile-device risk lifecycle: count of mobile-device risk register entries, treatment action items, owners, and remediation/closure timestamps.
data_signals: policy document record (version id, approval timestamp, approver id)
policy publication log or intranet publish timestamp and URL
employee acknowledgement receipts with timestamp and user id
policy review schedule entries and review minutes/attestation records
project plan artifacts indicating information security/mobile device sections (document id, project id, existence flag)
mobile device risk register entries (risk id, description, identified timestamp, severity)
risk treatment action items (owner, planned remediation date, remediation completion timestamp, status)
roles and responsibilities register showing assigned owners for mobile device security
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed/recorded policy approval and versioned publication logs plus automated employee acknowledgement receipts (user, timestamp) and documented project artifacts showing information-security sections.
Strong: risk register entries for mobile-device use with assigned owners and remediation completion timestamps demonstrating treatments closed.
Weak: ad-hoc email announcements without acknowledgement tracking, verbal approvals without versioned policy records, or isolated post-hoc project notes lacking documented security sections.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Risk language emphasizes defining, reviewing, approving, publishing and communicating policies which are preventive activities to ensure personnel understand requirements.

GOV-18.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the policy lifecycle, publication/communication and risk treatment specific to mobile devices (policy-level and procedural evidence) rather than technical enforcement metrics (e.g., device MDM enrollment); measurement must emphasize document metadata, acknowledgements, project-plan inclusion, and risk-register treatment status.

GOV-19.measurement_goal: Measure existence, formal approval and last-review currency of the Access Control Policy, coverage of policy publication/communication to relevant personnel and projects, and the percentage of organizational units/projects that reference or implement required segregation-of-duties and role/responsibility assignments derived from the policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive policy control, auditors need coverage and compliance ratios (percentage of org units with an approved/current policy & percentage of staff who received/acknowledged it); counts (documents, reviews) and trends (review frequency over time) supplement the view.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of applicable organizational units/projects with a current, approved Access Control Policy and documented review date; count of personnel who have received/acknowledged the policy; count of project plans referencing information security/access control; existence and coverage of segregation-of-duties (SoD) matrices and assigned responsibilities with approval timestamps.
data_signals: policy document repository entry for 'Access control policy' with version, approval timestamp and approver
policy review log / last-reviewed timestamp per policy
policy publication log or distribution list showing publish/share timestamps
employee policy acknowledgement receipts / communication delivery confirmations
project plan entries referencing 'information security' or 'access control policy' (document links or checklist flags)
role and responsibility matrix entries mapping access responsibilities with assignment timestamps
segregation-of-duties (SoD) matrix records and exception approvals
policy exceptions and approval records (exception id, approver, expiry)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable policy repository records with approval timestamps, signed acknowledgements from personnel, automated distribution logs, and documented SoD matrices referenced by project plans.
Weak: single emailed PDF without approval metadata, undocumented verbal communications, or ad-hoc project notes that do not link to an approved policy.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns creating, approving, publishing and communicating an Access Control Policy — actions that prevent misaligned behavior by defining requirements.

GOV-19.differentiation_note: This control focuses on policy lifecycle and communication (documentation, approvals, acknowledgements, SoD mapping) rather than technical enforcement metrics like access logs or entitlement provisioning coverage.

GOV-20.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of documented organizational context: percentage of projects incorporating information security requirements, percentage of roles with assigned information security responsibilities, currency and approval status of Information Security Policies, and count/recency of maintained contacts with external interest groups related to threats.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and aims to demonstrate coverage and currency (best expressed as percentages of projects, roles, and policies in-compliance) while counts (contacts, overdue items) and trends (policy review cadence over time) provide supporting context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active projects whose project plan includes a documented information security section or risk entry; percentage of organizational roles that have an assigned, recorded information security responsibility in the RACI/role matrix; timestamps and approval records for Information Security Policy documents showing last review/approval date and publication; count and last-contact date for maintained interest-group contact entries used for threat awareness.
data_signals: project plan records with 'security' section flag or security-risk field and project ID
role/responsibility matrix entries (role, assigned security responsibility, assignment date)
policy document metadata: creation date, last review date, approval signature/ID, publication timestamp
policy distribution/communication logs (email send IDs, distribution list, acknowledgement receipts)
registry of external interest-group contacts with last-contact timestamp and contact purpose
count of projects missing documented security tasks and list of those project IDs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable project templates and project management system records showing enforced security sections, documented approval signatures and review timestamps, and an auditable contacts registry with last-contact dates.
Moderate: scanned copies of approved policies with review dates and periodic manual reviews recorded in meeting minutes.
Weak: undated policy documents, informal email threads without approval metadata, or anecdotal claims that contacts exist without a maintained registry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The associated risk language focuses on failures to 'define, review, approve, publish, share, or communicate' policies and to allocate responsibilities — activities that prevent security gaps, indicating a preventive control.

GOV-20.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance-level evidence (policy currency, assigned responsibilities, external contacts and project inclusion) rather than operational controls; measurement must prove organizational coverage and communication rather than technical detections. Note: control description was empty, so taxonomy is derived from associated risk and scenario texts.

GOV-21.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented interested‑party needs and contacts, the proportion of information security policies that map to those needs and have formal approval/publication/communication records, and the percent of projects and roles where information security requirements and responsibilities are explicitly defined and allocated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage/adhesion metrics (percent of interested parties documented, percent of policies communicated). Counts (e.g., contact entries, policy approvals) and trends (regular review over time) supplement coverage; a score can aggregate multiple coverage checks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of relevant interested parties with a maintained contact record and documented needs/expectations; the percentage of information security policies that include mapping to interested‑party requirements plus recorded approval/publication and acknowledgement receipts; and the percentage of active projects with an information security section in the project plan and the percentage of defined roles with allocated information security responsibilities.
data_signals: registry of interested parties / interest groups with contact owner and last updated timestamp
documented interested‑party needs/requirements artifacts (requirements mapping files)
policy approval records and publication timestamps for Information Security Policies
policy distribution / acknowledgement receipts (employee acknowledgement logs, distribution lists)
project plans with information security sections or risk identification entries and associated timestamps
role/responsibility matrix entries showing defined and allocated information security responsibilities (RACI rows)
policy review cycle records (review dates) and communication logs to interest groups
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: maintained registries with last‑updated timestamps, linked policy approval records, and signed/recorded acknowledgement receipts showing active communication.
Strong: project plans instrumented to include a security checklist or risk assessment entry and regular review timestamps.
Weak: one‑off documents or ad hoc emails without authoritative registries, missing timestamps, or no acknowledgement receipts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control phrase 'The organization determines' indicates planning and definition activities (defining interested parties, policies, responsibilities), which are preventive in nature.

GOV-21.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on identifying and documenting interested‑party requirements and ensuring policies/projects/roles reflect and communicate those needs, rather than technical controls or incident detection; measurements must include stakeholder/contact registries and policy communication artifacts.

GOV-22.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and approved publication of the ISMS scope: percentage of organizational units, assets, projects and external interfaces included in the documented ISMS scope; presence and timeliness of scope approval and communication to interested parties; count of identified interested parties with contact records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and focuses on coverage and communication: auditors need coverage percentages (what portion of the organization/assets/projects are in scope) and counts (interested parties, approvals). Trends and comparisons show periodic review and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of business units, assets, projects and external interfaces explicitly listed in the ISMS scope document vs. approved organizational inventory; timestamped approval and publication records for the scope; count and contact records of identified interested parties and evidence of scope communication to them; presence of a roles/responsibilities matrix showing assigned ISMS responsibilities.
data_signals: documented ISMS scope statement (file/version/timestamp)
scope change log with approval timestamps and approver IDs
inventory of organizational units/assets/projects mapped to scope inclusion/exclusion flags
published information security policy or scope publication timestamp and distribution list
register of interested parties / contact list
roles and responsibilities matrix / RACI entries for ISMS responsibilities
project plans or change records showing inclusion/exclusion of information security
evidence of communications (email broadcast record, intranet publication, or meeting minutes) confirming scope
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a versioned ISMS scope document with signed/recorded approval, scope-to-asset mapping from the asset inventory, logged publication to a corporate distribution list, and an up-to-date RACI showing assigned ISMS responsibilities.
Weak: undated or draft scope notes, verbal confirmations, single one-off email without an authoritative publication record, or scope statements not mapped to assets/projects.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Determining the scope of the information security management system' describes establishing coverage and boundaries (a preventive activity) rather than detecting incidents.

GOV-22.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about documented ISMS scope, stakeholder identification and communication (coverage and approval evidence), differing from operational controls that measure technical defenses or incident detection. If a single focus_area is needed, 'governance_risk_compliance' is primary; other focus areas were added because associated risks emphasize contacts, projects and personnel understanding.

GOV-23.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of the information security policy lifecycle (definition, review, approval, publication, communication) and the organization-wide assignment of information security responsibilities — e.g., percent of required policies with current approval and employee acknowledgement, percent of active projects with a documented security risk assessment in the project plan, and percent of roles with recorded information-security responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control where auditors need coverage and compliance percentages (policy currency, acknowledgement rates, role assignment coverage); counts of documents/projects/roles and trends over time are useful secondary evidence of improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of required information security policy documents that are approved and reviewed within the policy-defined review period (policy document id, owner, approval_date, last_review_date). 2) Percentage of employees who have recorded policy acknowledgement receipts for current policies. 3) Percentage of active projects whose project plan contains a completed security risk assessment or security checklist artifact. 4) Percentage of organizational roles with defined and recorded information security responsibilities (RACI/HR role record).
data_signals: policy inventory records with owner, approval_date, last_review_date, version
policy publication records/intranet upload timestamps and distribution list
employee policy acknowledgement receipts (user id, policy id, timestamp)
management review / ISMS review meeting minutes with dates
project plan artifacts indicating security risk assessment attached (project id, artifact id, timestamp)
project change request or checklist entries showing security tasks completed
RACI / HR role-definition records showing assigned information security responsibilities and assignment date
internal audit reports or nonconformity tickets referencing policy currency or role assignment
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy repository records (owner, approval_date, last_review_date) plus machine-readable employee acknowledgement receipts and project-management links demonstrating coverage.
Strong: consistent trend showing >95% of policies reviewed within policy-defined periods and increasing % of projects with attached risk assessments over time.
Weak: single point-in-time PDF snapshots or emailed policy copies without timestamps or acknowledgement logs.
Weak: informal statements or undocumented role responsibilities (no RACI/HR record) and absence of project artifacts linking security work to plans.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The associated risks and text focus on defining/reviewing/approving/publishing/communicating policies and assigning responsibilities — activities that prevent policy gaps and unclear responsibilities.

GOV-23.differentiation_note: This control is governance-focused (policy lifecycle and role assignment) rather than a technical detection or remediation control; measurement must target policy metadata, employee acknowledgements, project-plan artifacts, and RACI/HR records rather than logs or vulnerability metrics. (Control description was not provided; taxonomy derived from associated risk texts.)

GOV-24.measurement_goal: Demonstrate leadership oversight by measuring the percentage of required information security policies that are defined, approved, published and communicated on schedule; the percentage of projects that include documented security risk activities in project plans; and the share/count of roles with explicitly assigned information security responsibilities and tracked segregation-of-duty conflicts.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control — auditors need coverage and compliance rates (percentages) such as percent of policies reviewed/published and percent of projects with security tasks. Counts (policy documents, SoD conflicts), scores (maturity/leadership engagement), and trends over time are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of information security policies that have an approval timestamp, publication date, and recorded communication (e.g., distribution or acknowledgement) within the required review cycle; percentage of active projects whose project plan includes security risk identification and treatment entries; count/percentage of job roles with assigned information security responsibilities in the RACI or role registry; count of recorded segregation-of-duty conflicts and their resolution status and time-to-resolve.
data_signals: policy document repository entries with definition, approval and publication timestamps
policy distribution/communication logs and employee policy acknowledgement receipts
policy review schedule and last review dates (next-review due dates)
project plan artifacts indicating security tasks or risk treatment entries (project repository entries)
role/RACI registry entries mapping information security responsibilities to job titles
segregation-of-duties matrix and logged conflicts with resolution status and timestamps
records of policy-related communications (email/collaboration posts) linked to policy IDs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy repository records showing approval timestamps plus per-employee acknowledgement receipts and project plan entries with linked security tasks; maintained RACI registry and SoD matrix with logged conflicts and resolution timestamps.
Weak: one-off statements or PDF snapshots without approval metadata, manual attestations without timestamps, or undocumented verbal commitments from leadership.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title and associated risks focus on establishing and communicating policies and allocating responsibilities to prevent misunderstandings and operational failures, which is preventive in nature.

GOV-24.differentiation_note: This control focuses on leadership-level governance: policy lifecycle, formal communication/acknowledgement, project-plan integration, role assignment and SoD conflict tracking — measurement needs span policy and organizational records rather than technical detection or remediation metrics.

GOV-25.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, approval and publication status of the information security policy set by top management (count of required policy documents and percent approved/published), the timeliness of policy reviews (percent on-schedule), and the percent of relevant personnel or project artifacts that have documented acknowledgement or reference to the policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive, policy-establishment control so coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of policies approved/published, percent of staff who acknowledged) show whether the policy is in effect; counts and trends support inventory and review cadence visibility.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of mandated information security policy documents that are present in the policy repository with documented top-management approval, publication timestamp, current version and next-review date; percentage of employees (or role-groups) who have acknowledged the current policy version; count/percentage of active project plans that reference the information security policy and number of policies overdue for scheduled review.
data_signals: policy repository records: policy id, title, version, approval timestamp, approver (top management)
publication metadata (intranet/document store publish timestamp, URL)
policy review schedule entries and last review date
employee policy acknowledgement logs (user id, timestamp, policy version acknowledged)
policy-to-project mapping or project plan references to information security policy (project id, reference flag)
list of assigned information security responsibilities (role -> responsibility mapping)
count of policies with status = draft | approved | published | retired
records of communication events about policy release (email distribution logs, training session logs referencing policy)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable policy repository with approval workflow logs, publication timestamps, and employee acknowledgement records that can be queried across time.
Strong: documented mapping showing policy inclusion in project plans and role/responsibility assignments.
Weak: single point-in-time signed PDF without publication/acknowledgement logs or no version/review metadata.
Weak: anecdotal or verbal claims of 'policy exists' without repository records or approval evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'Top management shall establish an information security policy' describes a governance action intended to prevent gaps, so it is preventive.

GOV-25.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about establishment, approval, publication and communication of the top-level information security policy (existence, approval and acknowledgements), rather than operational controls (e.g., technical enforcement or incident handling) — measurement must focus on policy artifacts, approvals, review cadence and communication evidence.

GOV-26.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented organizational roles, responsibilities and authorities for information security: percent of required roles with documented responsibilities and approved authorities, count/percent of active projects with an assigned security owner, and percent of personnel who have acknowledged their information security responsibilities and related policies.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control — auditors need percent coverage (e.g., % of roles documented/authorized and % of projects with security owner) and counts/trends to show completeness and ongoing maintenance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of role entries in the organization's role/responsibility registry and the percentage of those entries that have: an approved authority statement, approval timestamp, and a recent review date; count and percentage of active projects with a populated security-owner field in the project plan; count and percentage of personnel who have acknowledged relevant information security policies and their role-specific responsibilities.
data_signals: role-responsibility registry entries (role_id, responsibilities_text, authority_statement, effective_date)
policy document metadata (policy_id, version, approval_timestamp, approver)
policy publication/communication logs (publication_date, distribution_list, communication_channel)
policy acknowledgment receipts by employee (employee_id, policy_id, acknowledgment_timestamp)
project plan metadata with security owner field (project_id, security_owner_id, project_plan_last_updated)
role allocation/change logs (role_id, assigned_person_id, assignment_timestamp, assignment_approver)
training/compliance assignment and completion records tied to roles (role_id, course_id, completion_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated role registry entries with approval timestamps, signed policy approval records, machine-readable project plan fields populated for security owner, and organization-wide acknowledgment receipts with timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc email statements, manually compiled spreadsheets without timestamps or approver fields, or occasional attestations without linkage to project plans.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns defining and allocating roles, responsibilities and authorities (proactive organizational controls) which prevent unclear responsibilities and policy communication failures.

GOV-26.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls, this control requires measurement of documented organizational artifacts (role registry, policy approvals, project plan assignments and acknowledgments) rather than system logs; because the control description was not provided, keywords and signals are drawn from associated risk text.

GOV-27.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and completion of documented actions that address identified information security risks and opportunities: percent of risks with approved treatment plans implemented within defined timeframes; percent of information security policies that have been defined, reviewed, approved, published and acknowledged; percent of projects that include documented security risk treatment tasks; and percent of roles with assigned security responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage/control-implementation control — auditors need percentage coverage (e.g., % risks with treatment, % policies reviewed/communicated). Counts and trends are useful secondary measures to show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of entries in the risk register that have a documented treatment action, approval timestamp, assigned owner, and a completion/remediation timestamp (or current status). Percent of active information security policy documents with version history, approval metadata, publication date and acknowledgement records. Percent of projects containing a documented security risk task linked to the risk register. Percent of roles with a recorded definition/assignment for information security responsibility.
data_signals: risk register entries with treatment_action, owner, status, created_at, approved_at, completed_at
policy documents metadata: version history, approval timestamps, publisher, published_date
policy distribution/acknowledgement records (employee signature/ACK timestamps or training completion tied to policy)
project plan artifacts with security risk task flags and linkage IDs to risk register entries
RACI / role assignment records indicating assigned information security responsibilities
remote work security assessment reports and remediation logs for sites
mobile device inventory records, risk assessments and MDM enrollment/compliance status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked, timestamped artifacts (risk register entries linked to treatment tasks, approvals, and completion timestamps) and automated acknowledgement logs showing publication and receipt.
Strong: project artifacts that include traceability from project tasks to the central risk register and remediation evidence.
Weak: verbal attestations, undated document drafts, or one-off remediation emails without linkage to a risk register or approval metadata.
Weak: annual/point-in-time reports without trend/history showing ongoing treatment and communications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title and associated risks focus on defining and implementing actions (policies, role definitions, project inclusion) to prevent security lapses, indicating a preventive control.

GOV-27.differentiation_note: This control is broad and cross-cutting — unlike operational controls that measure single technical defenses, GOV-27 requires cross-domain coverage metrics (policy lifecycle, risk register treatment, project integration, role assignment, remote/mobile assessments); the control description was empty, so measurement needs were inferred from the linked risk descriptions and indicators.

GOV-28.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of required ISMS documented information: percentage of required policies/documents that exist, have an approval record and publication timestamp, and have been communicated to relevant personnel; plus the percent of project plans that include required information-security references and the percent of roles with assigned security responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and adherence (existence, approval, communication) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rate) best show whether required documentation and communications meet policy; counts show absolute missing items and trends show review cadence over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of ISMS-required documents/policies that have (a) a recorded approval (approver ID and timestamp), (b) a publication/push-to-production or intranet posting timestamp, and (c) at least one communication or acknowledgement record tied to personnel. Also measure the count and percentage of active project plans that reference information security requirements and the count/percentage of organizational roles with a recorded information-security responsibility assignment (RACI).
data_signals: document inventory listing ISMS documents and policy identifiers
document approval records (approver identity and approval timestamp)
document revision history and last_review_date
publication/distribution logs or intranet posting timestamps
communication or acknowledgement records (emails, read-receipts, policy-acknowledgement entries, training completions)
project plan artifacts or repository entries containing security sections or security checklist references
RACI / role assignment records showing allocated information security responsibilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated document inventory with approval workflow metadata, publication timestamps, and user acknowledgement logs; project management records with explicit security checklist entries and linked evidence.
Weak: single point-in-time PDF snapshots, manually collected spreadsheets without timestamps, or anecdotal statements that policies exist without approver or distribution metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'shall include' and the emphasis on documented information indicate establishing requirements and documentation to prevent gaps, which is a preventive control.

GOV-28.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the existence, approval, publication and communication of ISMS documents and their integration into project plans and role assignments — unlike sibling controls that might focus solely on technical controls or incident processes, GOV-28 requires measuring document lifecycle metadata, communication/acknowledgement evidence, and coverage across projects and roles.

GOV-29.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of documented information (policies, procedures, records) created or updated that have required lifecycle controls (owner, approval, version, review date, publication/distribution evidence) and count exceptions where required review/approval/publishing steps were not completed within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, counts of nonconforming document events (e.g., creations/updates without approval) directly show failures; percentage and trend help show coverage and improvement over time against policy requirements for document lifecycle steps (review/approve/publish).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of documents created/updated in the audit window and, for each document, the presence and values of lifecycle metadata: owner ID, approver ID and approval timestamp, version identifier, scheduled review date and actual review completion timestamp, publication/distribution timestamp, and any acknowledgement receipts. Also measure counts of documents missing one or more required lifecycle fields and counts of approvals completed after policy SLA.
data_signals: document metadata records (owner, version, creation timestamp, last modified timestamp)
document approval workflow logs (approver ID, approval timestamp, approval status)
document review schedule vs. review completion timestamps
publication/distribution records (publication timestamp, distribution list or share action logs)
policy acknowledgement receipts or training acknowledgement tied to the document
change request or revision IDs linked to document updates
count of documents missing owner or approval
exceptions where approval or review occurred after policy SLA / overdue review entries
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated document management system audit logs showing approver IDs, timestamps, version history, and distribution receipts for each create/update event.
Strong: workflow completion records with SLA timestamps and recorded acknowledgements.
Weak: ad-hoc saved copies or emailed PDFs without approval timestamps, manual screenshots of approval, or a one-time spreadsheet inventory with no audit trail.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels the control as detective and the wording 'When creating and updating ... ensure appropriate' implies verification/verification checks (reviews/approvals) rather than purely preventive configuration.

GOV-29.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets lifecycle controls and evidence for create/update events (owners, approvals, publication/distribution and review timeliness), distinct from sibling controls that might focus on policy content, training uptake, or assignment of responsibilities.

GOV-30.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of required information-security documents that are defined, have an assigned owner, have an approved current version published, have completed scheduled reviews within policy timeframes, and have been communicated/acknowledged by applicable personnel; additionally measure the count of project plans missing documented security requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance (e.g., percentage of documents with current approved versions and acknowledgements). Counts (number of missing/overdue items) and trends (review completion over time) supplement coverage metrics to show velocity and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required Information Security Policies and related documented information listed in the document register that: have an assigned owner, have an approval record (approver id and approval timestamp), are published (publication timestamp and distribution list), have a last-review and next-review date within policy intervals, and have employee acknowledgement records where applicable. Also measure the count and percentage of active project plans that include a documented security section or risk-treatment entry.
data_signals: document register entries (document id, title, owner, classification, required review frequency)
approval records (approver id, approval timestamps, approval version)
version history and publication timestamps for each document
next-review and last-review date fields from document metadata
employee policy attestation/acknowledgement logs with timestamps and user IDs
project plan artifacts or metadata indicating presence/absence of an information security section (project id, security section present flag)
access/publication distribution lists and policy access logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated document management system metadata with structured fields (owner, approval timestamp, version, next-review date) and machine-readable acknowledgement logs.
Strong: project management repository entries that include an explicit security section or linked risk-treatment artifact.
Weak: ad-hoc email chains or PDF files without metadata, manual attestations without timestamps, or one-off snapshots (point-in-time) that don't show recurring review adherence.

affinity_keywords: risks are not identified and treated

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control aims to ensure policies and documented information are defined, approved, published and communicated to prevent personnel misunderstanding and gaps in project security coverage.

GOV-30.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets the documentation lifecycle (definition, approval, publication, scheduled review and communication/acknowledgement) and verification of security coverage in project plans, rather than technical controls or incident processes; analysis relies on document metadata and acknowledgement records (control description was not provided, so associated risk text guided keyword selection).

GOV-31.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of ISMS monitoring activities: percent of information security policies reviewed/published/communicated on schedule, percent of projects with documented security risk assessments, percent of roles with assigned information‑security responsibilities, and coverage of remote‑work endpoints under monitoring or configuration baselines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about governance-level monitoring and coverage (policy reviews, project inclusion, role assignment, endpoint coverage) where percentage/coverage metrics (e.g., % policies reviewed, % projects with security included) most directly show compliance; counts and trends are useful secondary signals to show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate coverage and timeliness metrics that an auditor can query: (1) percentage of active information security policies with a recorded review date within the defined review cycle and an approval/publication timestamp; (2) count and percentage of projects in the project repository that include a documented security risk assessment or security requirements section; (3) percentage of staff roles with a recorded information‑security responsibility assignment (RACI/role profile); (4) percentage of remote endpoints inventoried and reporting baseline security/monitoring telemetry.
data_signals: policy review/approval timestamps and reviewer IDs from policy management system
policy publication/distribution logs and publication version history
policy acknowledgement/comms/training completion records for personnel
project plan metadata indicating presence/absence of security section or attached risk assessment artifact
role description or RACI records showing assigned information security responsibilities
remote endpoint inventory with configuration/compliance scan results
remote access (VPN/SSO) connection logs showing monitored endpoints
monitoring/telemetry agent presence and heartbeat records for remote devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated system records (policy management timestamps, project repository metadata, role registry, endpoint telemetry) showing continuous coverage and recent review dates; dashboards showing trending improvement.
Weak: single manual attestation, ad‑hoc email approvals, or yearly spreadsheet snapshots without system-backed timestamps or project linkage.
Strong: integrated evidence linking project artifacts to security risk assessment documents rather than verbal/undocumented claims.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation' implies ongoing detection and assessment activities to surface gaps (detective), so classification is detective.

GOV-31.differentiation_note: This control is governance‑level monitoring focused on policy/project/role coverage and remote‑work endpoint monitoring rather than technical detection of threats; its unique need is measurement of review/approval/publish/assignment coverage and linkage to project artifacts and endpoint telemetry.

GOV-32.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of scheduled management review meetings completed on policy-defined cadence, the count and closure rate of management-review action items (with owners and due dates), and coverage of required agenda topics (policy review, risk treatment, contacts with interest groups, responsibilities) in review minutes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Management review is a preventive governance control where auditors expect coverage/closure rates (percentages) and counts of outstanding items; trending of completion and closure over time helps demonstrate ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of management review meetings scheduled vs. held within the policy-defined interval, number of action items opened by each review and their status (open/closed), time-to-closure per action item, and a binary/percent indicator per meeting whether required agenda topics (policy review, risk treatment, contacts, responsibilities, remote work risks) were documented in minutes. Measurements should reference meeting timestamps, attendance, documented agenda topics, action item owner and due date, and artifact links to approved policy updates or communications.
data_signals: management review calendar entries and meeting timestamps
meeting minutes/records listing agenda topics and decisions (including 'Information Security Policies', 'risk', 'contacts with interest groups', 'responsibilities')
action item register created from reviews: item id, owner, due date, status, closure timestamp
policy approval records and publication timestamps
communications logs evidencing policy sharing/publishing (email or intranet publish timestamps)
project plan records or change requests referencing security review outcomes
contact list of interest groups and engagement records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured meeting minutes with formal agenda, attendance list, linked action-item tracker with owners and closure timestamps, and published policy approval records; provides continuous traceability.
Weak: informal or verbal confirmations, undated notes, or ad-hoc emails without an action-tracking record; single annual statement without artifact links.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named 'Management review' and intended to ensure policies, responsibilities and risk treatment are maintained—a governance/preventive activity to avoid control failures.

GOV-32.differentiation_note: This entry focuses on governance artifacts (meeting schedule, minutes, action-item closure and policy publication) rather than technical controls — measurement must track periodic review completion and decisions rather than technical event telemetry.

GOV-33.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and timeliness of continual improvement activities (nonconformities, corrective actions, policy updates, management-review actions) and the percentage completed/closed within target timeframes; additionally measure the proportion of projects incorporating information security requirements and the frequency of policy reviews/communications to personnel.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Continual improvement is a management/process control where coverage and closure rates matter (percent complete/on-time). Counts of actions and trend over time supplement percentage metrics to show volume and improvement trajectory.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of identified improvement opportunities (audit findings, nonconformities, corrective actions, policy change requests, security gaps in project plans) per period; percentage of those actions resolved/closed within the defined target timeframe; number and timing of formal management review meetings and policy review/communication events; percentage of projects with documented security considerations in project plans.
data_signals: nonconformity / corrective action records with creation and closure timestamps
internal audit findings and remediation tickets (with severity)
policy version history and publish/approve timestamps
management review meeting minutes and action item lists
project plan metadata indicating inclusion of information security requirements
communications logs (policy distribution emails, acknowledgement receipts)
overdue improvement actions count and age distribution
root-cause analysis records linked to corrective actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated tracking records (ticketing system) showing timestamps for creation and closure of corrective actions are strong evidence.
Version-controlled policy repository entries and signed approval timestamps strengthen evidence of review/publish activity.
Systematic project plan metadata showing explicit security inclusion is strong; ad-hoc verbal notes or undocumented decisions are weak evidence.
Single-instance meeting minutes without linked tracked actions are weak; linked action items with closure proof are strong.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is about implementing continual improvement to prevent failures such as lack of defined/reviewed policies and unclear responsibilities (addresses prevention of policy/process deficiencies described in associated risk text).

GOV-33.differentiation_note: This control focuses on management-system level measurement (closure rates, policy review frequency, project-plan security inclusion) rather than technical detection or remediation metrics; it requires tracking of process artifacts (reviews, action items, policy versions) rather than system logs.

HRS-7.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented information security responsibilities by role: percentage of defined roles with a published job/role description that explicitly lists information security responsibilities, the count of active roles without allocations, and timestamps of last approval/communication for each role.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completeness (so percentage of roles defined is primary). Counts of missing or undefined roles and trending of coverage over time are useful secondary signals to show remediation and maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of roles in the authoritative role inventory that have a documented information security responsibilities section, an approving authority and a publication/communication timestamp; count of active employees assigned to roles lacking such documentation; and the date of last review per role to show currency.
data_signals: authoritative role inventory entries (role_id, role_name, role_owner)
job/role description documents containing 'information security' responsibilities field
approval records and publication timestamps for each role description
role-to-employee assignment mappings (employee_id -> role_id)
acknowledgement or distribution logs showing responsibilities communicated (signed receipts, intranet distribution timestamps)
role review/audit logs with last review date and reviewer
termination/exit checklist entries indicating confidentiality commitments communicated
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized role inventory + published job descriptions with approval timestamps and automated distribution/acknowledgement receipts.
Strong: linkage between role entries and employee assignments proving allocations.
Weak: verbal confirmations, ad-hoc spreadsheets without approval timestamps, or annual narrative reports without per-role documentation.
Weak: absence of review timestamps or evidence of communication to incumbents.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'shall be defined and allocated' indicates the control prevents issues by ensuring responsibilities are established before activity occurs.

HRS-7.differentiation_note: This control targets completeness and allocation of role-level security responsibilities (documentation, approval, communication) rather than background checks, training delivery, or technical access controls — measurement must focus on role documentation coverage and assignment linkage.

HRS-8.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of user accounts and roles that comply with the organization's segregation-of-duties (SoD) policy: percent of roles with no incompatible role-pair assignments, count of active SoD conflicts, and number of documented, approved SoD exceptions and their compensating controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Segregation-of-duties is primarily a preventive coverage/control compliance requirement — auditors want coverage percentages (e.g., % of roles with conflicts) and counts of conflict instances; a compliance score is useful to summarize policy adherence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percentage of user-role assignments that violate the SoD matrix (incompatible role pairs), count of distinct users with at least one SoD conflict, number of active SoD exceptions with approval metadata, and evidence of periodic SoD/role review completion (timestamps and reviewer IDs). Include role-to-permission mapping and privileged account lists to scope impact.
data_signals: role-to-permission mapping export
user-to-role assignment list with timestamps
defined incompatible role-pair (SoD) matrix
record of approved SoD exceptions (approver, approval date, compensating control)
access/role review completion timestamps and attestation records
privileged account inventory and role tags
provisioning/deprovisioning approval logs (who approved, when)
policy document version and publication/communication timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated exports from IAM systems showing current user-role assignments and system-evaluated SoD conflict counts (strong).
Documented, system-linked SoD exception records with approver identity and expiry date (strong).
Periodic attestation logs proving reviews ran on schedule (strong).
One-off spreadsheets or screenshots without timestamps or approver identity (weak).
Ad hoc or undocumented manual approvals without compensating control evidence (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is focused on establishing SoD to prevent conflicts of interest, which is a preventive measure (description and associated risks reference 'Segregation of duties has not been established resulting in conflicts').

HRS-8.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of role/permission mappings and active SoD conflicts and exceptions (technical/record evidence), unlike sibling HR controls that may focus on background checks or termination communication which require different evidence types (e.g., checklists, exit attestations).

HRS-9.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented management responsibilities and related HR/security processes: percent of roles with approved information-security responsibilities, percent of new hires with proportionate background verification completed, percent of terminations with signed confidentiality commitments, and count of projects with a named information-security owner in project plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage and compliance across roles and HR processes, so percentage metrics (coverage/completion rates) best express compliance; counts and trends supplement to show volume and change over time (e.g., number of missing role definitions, background-check completion counts, change in coverage).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active job roles that have up-to-date, approved information security responsibility statements (including approval timestamp), percent of hires with background verification status recorded and proportional to role classification, percent of terminations with signed confidentiality commitments recorded, and count/percent of projects that include an assigned information security responsibility in the project plan.
data_signals: role responsibility document records with approval timestamps and version history
HR background verification status per hire (verification type, date, result) mapped to role information-classification
signed confidentiality/NDAs or termination clearance records with dates
policy register entries for Information Security Policies with define/review/approve/publish timestamps
project plan artifacts that include named information-security owners or security task assignments
segregation of duties matrix or role-assignment ledger showing conflicting assignments
training/awareness completion records and attestations for staff (dates, course IDs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative system records (HRIS, policy registry, project management tool) with approval timestamps and automated mappings between role classification and background-check status
Strong: signed confidentiality agreements stored in HR system with termination workflow evidence
Weak: ad-hoc emails or undocumented verbal confirmations of responsibilities
Weak: one-off spreadsheets without versioning or timestamps that don't demonstrate review/approval processes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on defining and communicating management responsibilities (prevention of role ambiguity and insider risk), as reflected by associated risks like 'Information security responsibilities have not been defined or allocated.'

HRS-9.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets documented management responsibilities, HR verification and termination confidentiality processes (policy and HR artifacts), rather than technical controls — measurement must focus on HRIS/policy/project records and evidence of communication and approvals; note: control description was not provided, so taxonomy is derived from associated risk texts.

HRS-10.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of new hires and contractors for whom background verification checks appropriate to the role and information classification were completed before or at the time of access provisioning, and count outstanding or overdue checks by role and information classification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Screening is a preventive control that requires coverage verification (percentage of hires screened); counts of unscreened hires and trend over hiring periods support investigation and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of new employees and contractors with completed background verification records and signed confidentiality acknowledgements mapped to their role/information classification prior to access provisioning, plus the count of hires where access was granted before checks completed. Include timestamps for background check completion, hire date, access grant, and role classification to enable SLA and timeliness analysis.
data_signals: background verification completion records with timestamp and scope (criminal, identity, reference checks)
HR hire/contractor start date and employee ID
role assignment and information classification mapping for the role
access provisioning event timestamps (first access granted)
signed confidentiality/non-disclosure agreement records and termination confidentiality acknowledgements
records of outstanding or overdue background checks and remediation actions
policy acknowledgement timestamps for employment/security policies
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/Identity system records showing background check completion timestamps before access provisioning and role-to-information-classification mapping.
Strong: signed confidentiality agreements and system-recorded policy acknowledgements linked to employee IDs.
Weak: ad-hoc email confirmations or paper checklists without timestamps, or annual manual attestations that do not prove checks occurred before access was granted.
Weak: spot-check samples without coverage metrics by role or information classification.

affinity_keywords: Failure to define, review, approve, publish, share, or communicate Information Security Policies

control_type_classification.reasoning: Described risks emphasize 'Background verification checks are not carried out' which indicates the control's purpose is to prevent insider threats by performing pre-employment checks.

HRS-10.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets pre-employment/role-proportional background checks and evidence tying check completion to access provisioning and information-classification mapping, unlike sibling controls that may focus on termination, training, or policy publication.

HRS-11.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and completeness of employment agreements: percentage of employees and contractors with signed agreements that include confidentiality commitments, defined information security responsibilities, asset use/disposal clauses, and proportionate background-verification requirements; and the percentage of terminations where confidentiality obligations were explicitly communicated and acknowledgement recorded.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about coverage and completeness of contractual terms, so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) show how many employees meet the requirement; counts and rates (e.g., number of hires with background checks, rate of terminations with acknowledgements) and trends show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of active employees and recent hires with a current signed employment agreement containing required clauses (confidentiality, information security responsibilities, asset handling/disposal), count and percentage of hires with completed background/verifications at the required level for their role, and count and percentage of terminations with documented communication of confidentiality commitments and recorded acknowledgements.
data_signals: signed employment agreement records (employee id, signature timestamp, template/version id)
employment agreement clause flags (confidentiality, security responsibilities, asset handling/disposal) or clause text presence
background verification records (completion status, verification level, completion date) linked to hire id
HRIS hire record and hire role/classification to determine proportional verification requirement
termination/exit checklist entries showing confidentiality commitments communicated and acknowledgement receipts
exit interview notes or signed exit confidentiality acknowledgement (employee id, timestamp)
policy/policy-template approval and publish dates (Information Security Policies) referenced by agreement template
distribution/acknowledgement logs (email or portal) for terms and policy communications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable signed agreements in HRIS with clause presence flags and timestamps, background-check records linked to hire records, and automated termination checklist entries with stored acknowledgements.
Moderate: scanned signed PDFs stored in a centralized repository with metadata showing signature date but lacking clause flags or linkage to HRIS.
Weak: existence of a policy document or template alone without per-employee signed agreements, or ad-hoc/manual attestations without timestamps or linkage to hire/termination events.

affinity_keywords: acquire, use, or dispose of assets

control_type_classification.reasoning: Terms and conditions establish preventive contractual obligations (e.g., confidentiality commitments, background verification requirements) to reduce insider and policy non-compliance risks, as reflected in associated risk descriptions.

HRS-11.differentiation_note: This control is focused on contractual coverage and HR lifecycle artifacts (hire and termination records) rather than technical controls—measurement requires HRIS/document evidence and linkage to background-check results; note: original control text was not provided so associated risk texts were used to derive keywords and signals.

HRS-12.measurement_goal: Measure the volume, timeliness, and outcomes of HR disciplinary investigations and sanctions for information-security related violations (including termination communications), and the proportion of security incidents or policy breaches that result in documented disciplinary action or corrective measures.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control tracks corrective/enforcement activity (completion rates and timeliness of disciplinary actions) so rate metrics (e.g., percent of investigations closed within target, sanction-per-incident rate) are most actionable; counts and percentages supplement volume and coverage views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of HR disciplinary investigations opened for information security policy violations, count and types of sanctions applied, time from incident detection to disciplinary outcome, proportion of security incidents mapped to HR actions, and presence of signed confidentiality/termination acknowledgements. Include background-verification completion flags for hires where relevant and policy acknowledgment records to show enforcement linkage.
data_signals: disciplinary action register / HR case records (employee ID, allegation type, investigation open/close timestamps, outcome, sanction type)
security incident records linked to employee IDs or case IDs showing policy-violation classification (breach → policy violation mapping)
termination checklist entries showing confidentiality commitments acknowledged (signed/dated) at exit
background verification completion status per hire
policy acknowledgment logs (employee signed/dated acknowledgements of information security policies)
training completion records and assessment results for security awareness
policy publication/approval logs and distribution records
counts of fines or regulatory notices associated with policy non-compliance (if any)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: linked, system-native HR case records with timestamps and case IDs tied to security incident IDs; signed termination confidentiality acknowledgements and completed background-check records.
Weak: ad-hoc email notes, undocumented verbal decisions, or isolated spreadsheet entries without incident linkage or timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The disciplinary process addresses violations and enforces sanctions to correct employee behavior and remediate policy non‑compliance, so it functions primarily as a corrective control.

HRS-12.differentiation_note: Unlike policy-creation or training controls, HRS-12 focuses on enforcement actions and outcome tracking (investigations, sanctions, termination acknowledgements and links to breaches), so measurements must come from HR case records and incident-to-HR linkage rather than solely training or policy publication logs.

HRS-13.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of employee terminations and role changes where access was revoked, assets returned, exit/termination confidentiality commitments recorded, and exit checklists completed within the organization's required timeframe; and count post-termination access or breaches attributable to former personnel.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (coverage/completion of offboarding activities), so a percentage (coverage/compliance rate of exit procedures) is most useful; counts and rates (number of failed deprovisions, post-termination access attempts) are useful secondary measures and trending shows improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of termination/role-change events with completed offboarding steps: (1) account disablement/role-change recorded within SLA, (2) asset return logged, (3) confidentiality/exit commitments recorded, and (4) exit checklist marked complete. Additionally, count post-termination access events and incidents attributed to former staff to show failures.
data_signals: HR termination/change event records with timestamps and employee identifiers
identity provider account disablement or role-change logs (timestamped)
access token/session revocation events and last-login timestamps
asset inventory return records (asset tag, return timestamp, condition)
signed confidentiality agreement / exit NDA record and timestamp
exit checklist completion records (who completed, timestamp)
audit/security logs showing post-termination access attempts (successful/failed) by former employee identifiers
incident reports referencing breaches or unauthorized access by former staff
