evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped system logs tying HR termination events to identity provider disablement and token revocation plus signed exit documents and asset-return records.
Strong: zero post-termination successful access events within monitoring logs for terminated accounts.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or verbal confirmation of access removal, or annual-only attestations without timestamps showing timely deprovisioning.
Weak: signed exit paperwork stored separately with no link to access-control logs or no proof of account disablement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control concerns termination/change procedures (preventing unauthorized access after role changes) and focuses on ensuring offboarding steps are completed to prevent breaches.

HRS-13.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets the offboarding lifecycle (deprovisioning, asset return, confidentiality at termination) and therefore requires measurement of HR events linked to identity and asset records—unlike sibling controls that may focus on onboarding, background checks, or policy publication.

HRS-14.measurement_goal: Measure coverage of acceptable‑use policy acknowledgements and asset handling controls (percentage of staff with signed acceptable‑use agreements, percentage of assets covered by approved handling/disposal procedures and MDM enrollment), plus counts/rates of acceptable‑use violations (unauthorized acquisition/use/disposal) and termination records showing confidentiality commitments communicated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive policy/control where auditors want coverage and adherence percentages (policy acknowledgement, asset coverage). Counts and rates are useful secondarily to track violations and incident frequency.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of active personnel with a timestamped signed acceptable‑use policy acknowledgement; 2) Percentage of company assets (by category: desktop/laptop/mobile) with assigned owner, documented handling/disposal procedure and MDM/enrollment status; 3) Count and rate of reported incidents of unauthorized acquisition/use/disposal of assets and confirmed breaches tied to acceptable‑use violations; 4) Percentage of terminations with recorded confidentiality commitments communicated and signed.
data_signals: signed acceptable‑use policy acknowledgements (user id, timestamp, version)
policy distribution logs and acknowledgement receipts
asset inventory records with owner, asset category, and disposal status
mobile device inventory and MDM enrollment/certificate status
asset disposal receipts / chain‑of‑custody logs
incident/issue tracker entries for unauthorized asset acquisition/use/disposal (timestamps, reporter, resolution)
termination checklist records showing confidentiality commitments communicated and signed
training completion records for acceptable‑use and security best practices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated acknowledgement records (user id + timestamp + policy version) and continuous MDM/enrollment logs tied to inventory; incident tickets with resolution and root cause that demonstrate enforcement.
Weak: one‑time mass email or screenshots of policy without per‑user acknowledgement, ad‑hoc Excel inventories, or anecdotal reports of disposal (no chain‑of‑custody).
Strong: termination records with signed confidentiality acknowledgement vs weak: absence of recorded termination communications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Acceptable use of assets defines policy and procedures to prevent unauthorized asset use/disposal and to ensure staff awareness, which is preventive in nature.

HRS-14.differentiation_note: Unlike HR screening or termination controls, this control specifically requires measurable coverage of acceptable‑use acknowledgements and asset handling (MDM/enrollment, disposal logs) — measurement must combine policy acknowledgement rates with asset inventory/enforcement signals.

HRS-15.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of personnel (employees, contractors, third parties) who have an up-to-date confidentiality / non-disclosure agreement (NDA) recorded in HR records, and track evidence that confidentiality commitments are communicated at termination and during role changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control — auditors want coverage percentages (what percent of relevant personnel have valid NDAs). Counts and trends are useful secondary metrics to show absolute numbers and changes over time (onboarding/termination cadence, overdue NDAs).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of active workforce (by employee/contractor/third‑party category and by role classification) with a recorded confidentiality or non‑disclosure agreement, plus counts of employees for whom confidentiality commitments were communicated at termination. Include NDA document identifier, signing/record date, associated role/access classification, and termination acknowledgement where applicable.
data_signals: record of confidentiality / non-disclosure agreements (document ID) linked to personnel records
NDA execution timestamp (signing or HR-recorded date)
personnel category (employee, contractor, third-party) and role / information classification mapping
onboarding checklist entries showing NDA completed
termination checklist entries showing confidentiality commitments communicated/acknowledged at exit
exceptions or waiver register for missing NDAs
incident / breach logs referencing disclosure by former or current personnel
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative HR system records linking personnel IDs to NDA document IDs and timestamps, with termination checklist entries and no open exceptions.
Strong: automated onboarding/termination workflow evidence showing NDA assignment and completion.
Weak: ad-hoc scanned PDFs without metadata, manual attestations without personnel linkage, or one-time policy documents (no per-person records).
Weak: absence of termination acknowledgement records when risks mention post‑employment disclosure.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Named control is about NDAs and confidentiality commitments which prevent unauthorized disclosure; associated risks emphasize preventing breaches at termination and during employment.

HRS-15.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on lifecycle coverage and communication of confidentiality commitments (onboarding and termination evidence) rather than related HR controls like background verification or role definition; measurement must show per-person NDA coverage and termination acknowledgements rather than only policy existence.

HRS-16.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of personnel competence and assurance activities: percentage of personnel with role‑aligned security training completed on schedule, percentage of new hires with background verification completed per policy, percentage of terminations with documented confidentiality commitments, and percentage of projects with assigned/ documented information security responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completion (e.g., proportion of staff with required training, background checks, and documented commitments), so percentage metrics best express coverage; counts and rates supplement with raw volumes and timeliness and a score can represent overall competence posture.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) percent of employees with required role-aligned security training completed within the policy timeframe; (b) percent of new hires with background verification checks completed before or within the onboarding SLA; (c) percent of terminated personnel with documented confidentiality commitments/exit acknowledgements; and (d) percent of active projects that include documented information security responsibilities.
data_signals: LMS training completion records (user id, course id, completion timestamp, role alignment tag)
HR onboarding records with background_verification_completed flag and completion timestamp
signed confidentiality agreement / exit acknowledgement records with employee id and termination date
policy publication and communication logs (publication date, distribution list or acknowledgement receipts)
project documentation records indicating assigned information security responsibilities (project id, role assigned, timestamp)
exceptions or non-compliance registers showing overdue or missing checks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/LMS records with timestamps and role mapping showing completed background checks and training for 100% of in-scope personnel.
Strong: signed confidentiality agreements tied to termination events in the HR system.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or undated attestations without timestamps or linked employee IDs.
Weak: one-off attestations (e.g., annual statement) without evidence of per-hire or per-termination enforcement and project-level assignment records.

affinity_keywords: staff are not aware of security threats

control_type_classification.reasoning: Classified as preventive because the control ensures personnel competence, background verification and communication of confidentiality/policies to prevent breaches and operational impacts (supported by associated risk phrases like 'Background verification checks' and 'Confidentiality commitments').

HRS-16.differentiation_note: Unlike technical or process controls, this control's measurements focus on HR and training artefacts (background checks, LMS completions, signed confidentiality records, project role assignments); note the control description is sparse, so associated risk texts were used to derive specific measurement signals.

IAC-14.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and compliance of network- and network-service-level access controls: percentage of network endpoints and services enforcing approved access policies, count/rate of unauthorized or denied network access attempts, and timeliness of revoking network access after entitlement changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive access control: coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., percent of devices enforcing NAC or services requiring authentication) show preventive effectiveness; counts and rates (unauthorized attempts, denies) and trends help detect gaps and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percent of network devices and services with active, policy-aligned access controls (NAC, firewall rules, service authentication) and the count/rate of unauthorized/denied network access attempts. Measure time delta between account termination or entitlement change and network access revocation to verify timely deprovisioning.
data_signals: network access control (NAC) enforcement logs showing device and user enforcement status
VPN and remote access authentication logs with user, device, timestamp, and result (success/fail)
802.1X or radius authentication success/failure events for wired/wireless networks
firewall and ACL accept/deny logs with source/destination and rule IDs
inventory of network services and mapped allowed principals/roles (service access list)
user provisioning/offboarding events and corresponding network account disable timestamps
network device configuration snapshots showing segmentation and access-rule application
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, time-stamped NAC and authentication logs correlated with provisioning events are strong evidence.
Synchronized timestamps across auth, NAC, and provisioning systems (allowing verification of timely revocation) increase strength.
Periodic manual attestations, single screenshots, or undated configuration exports are weak evidence for ongoing enforcement.

affinity_keywords: Access to networks and network services

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on controlling access to networks and services (preventing unauthorized access), which is characteristic of preventive controls.

IAC-14.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets network- and network-service-level access enforcement (NAC, VPN, firewall/ACLs and deprovisioning timing), as opposed to sibling controls that might focus on application-level access, physical access, or logging/detection.

IAC-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user accounts created with documented authorization and the percentage de-registered/deactivated within the policy-defined timeframe after employment/contract termination; also count active accounts without a valid owner or approval record.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: IAC-15 is preventive (controls over registration/de-registration require coverage and timeliness), so percentage metrics (coverage / SLA compliance) show policy adherence; counts and rates (e.g., orphan accounts, deprovisioning rate) provide operational detail and trends for improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count new user account creation events and link each to an approval/provisioning request ID and approver; count deactivation events and measure time delta between HR termination (or contract end) timestamp and account deactivation timestamp; count active accounts lacking an approval record or account owner. Group results by account type (employee/contractor/service/privileged) to apply differing policy timeframes.
data_signals: user account creation event records (user_id, creation_timestamp, created_by, approval_request_id)
account deactivation / disabled event records (user_id, deactivation_timestamp, reason)
HR termination or contract end records (employee_id, termination_date)
provisioning/approval request records (request_id, approver_id, approval_timestamp)
active account inventory (user_id, account_status, owner/manager, last_activity_timestamp, privilege_level)
privileged account list / flag (user_id, privilege_tag)
exceptions or manual deprovisioning tickets (ticket_id, resolver, resolution_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated provisioning and deprovisioning logs that include approval_request_id linked to HR termination events and timestamps showing deactivation within policy SLAs.
Strong: scheduled reports showing percentage of accounts deactivated within policy timeframes and counts of orphan/active-without-owner over time.
Weak: one-off spreadsheets or screenshots of account lists without timestamps or links to approval records; manual lists that cannot be reconciled to HR events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on controlling user registration and de-registration to prevent unauthorized access, which is a preventive lifecycle control.

IAC-15.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on the provisioning/deprovisioning lifecycle (authorization at creation and timely de-registration) rather than ongoing authentication or logging; measurement must link account events to approval/HR records — description was minimal so keywords and risks were used to define scope.

IAC-16.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of user account provisioning and deprovisioning: percentage of provisioning requests completed with approved authorizations within SLA, count of orphan or unapproved accounts, and median time-to-provision/deprovision by account type (including privileged accounts).
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Provisioning is a preventive control where auditors want coverage/compliance rates (percentage of requests meeting approval and SLA); counts and rates (request volumes, time-to-provision) are useful secondary metrics to show workload and timeliness, and trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute (a) percent of provisioning and deprovisioning requests that have an associated approved authorization and a completed execution timestamp within the defined SLA, grouped by account type (standard, privileged, service); (b) count of active accounts without a linked provisioning request or owner (orphan accounts); and (c) median and P90 time-to-provision and time-to-deprovision for requests over the audit period.
data_signals: access provisioning request records (request_id, requester, requested_roles, requested_timestamp)
approval/authorization logs (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_decision)
account lifecycle events (account_created_timestamp, account_disabled_timestamp, account_deleted_timestamp)
provisioning execution logs/automation run results (job_id, success/failure, error_reason)
account metadata with owner/manager attribute (to identify orphan accounts)
privileged account creation/change events and assigned role/entitlements
time-to-provision/time-to-deprovision per request (derived from request vs execution timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: end-to-end automated provisioning logs linking request_id → approval → execution with timestamps and account IDs, and instrumentation showing SLA compliance over multiple periods.
Strong: role/owner metadata present for all accounts enabling detection of orphan accounts and privileged assignments.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or screenshots of approvals, or point-in-time manual attestations without timestamps linking request→approval→execution.
Weak: manual one-off examples of provisioning without representative sample or trend data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'User access provisioning' describes a preventive lifecycle control that establishes and enforces correct account creation, assignment and removal to prevent unauthorized access (preventive intent).

IAC-16.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the provisioning lifecycle (requests, approvals, execution, SLAs, orphan accounts) rather than downstream activities like periodic access reviews or authentication monitoring; measurement must capture request→approval→execution linkage and timeliness.

IAC-17.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and control of privileged access allocation and use: percent of privileged accounts with documented, approved allocation records and recent attestation, count of privileged grants implemented without approval, and frequency of privileged-account usage events that deviate from authorized patterns.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., % privileged accounts with approval/attestation). Counts and rates (unauthorized grants, usage events) supplement with event volumes and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Quantify privileged-account population and verify each has an approved allocation record and a recent access attestation; measure number and rate of privileged access grants implemented without documented approval; measure privileged-account usage anomalies and link any usage to authorization records. Track time-to-revoke for deprecated privileged allocations.
data_signals: inventory of privileged accounts and privileged access rights assignments (account id, role, scope)
approval/provisioning request records and timestamps for each privileged grant
access attestation / certification completion records and timestamps
privileged account last-review date and reviewer identity
privileged-session logs (session start/end, user, target, commands or actions)
exception or emergency privileged access grant records
unauthorized privileged usage or access-denied events tied to privileged accounts
revocation/deprovisioning timestamps for privileged accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated PAM/privileged-account system logs + approval workflow records showing mapped request → approval → provisioning and periodic attestations.
Strong: continuous privileged-session recording and correlation to approval records.
Weak: one-time spreadsheet of privileged accounts without approval timestamps, or manual attestations without traceable provisioning logs.
Weak: post-incident forensic notes alone without ongoing approval/provisioning evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates that allocation and use of privileged access rights 'shall be restricted and controlled', which is a preventive requirement to stop unauthorized privileged access.

IAC-17.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets the allocation and ongoing use of privileged rights (approval, attestation, deprovisioning, and privileged-session evidence) rather than general user access controls, so measurement must focus on privileged-account inventories, approval/provisioning trails, and privileged-session telemetry.

IAC-18.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of active user accounts whose secret authentication information is managed according to policy (stored encrypted/hashed, rotated within required windows, and revoked on termination), plus counts and timeliness of secret-related incidents and credential revocations to demonstrate effective lifecycle management.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about coverage/compliance (best shown as % of accounts meeting secret-management policy). Counts and rates (incidents, revocations) and trend help show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of active user accounts that meet secret-management policy (e.g., credential storage flagged as hashed/encrypted, last rotation timestamp within policy window, and no plaintext secrets in associated artifacts). Also measure count of credential issuance/resets, number of credential compromise incidents, and time-to-revoke credentials after termination.
data_signals: user account inventory with credential storage attribute (hashed/encrypted/plaintext flag)
credential last-rotation timestamps per account
password/secret issuance and reset logs with timestamps and actor
account disablement/revocation events correlated with employee termination timestamps
secrets-in-source-code / repository scan findings (file, repo, secret detected, scan timestamp)
incident records tagged as credential compromise or breach with root-cause and timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated system records showing hashed/encrypted storage flags and timestamped rotation events (strong).
Continuous issuance/revocation logs that correlate revocation to termination dates within required windows (strong).
One-off manual attestations or annual reports without timestamps (weak).
Periodic source-code secret-scan findings and remediation tickets showing closure times (strong); infrequent or missing scans (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title focuses on 'management' of secret authentication information to prevent unauthorized access, which is inherently preventive (controls applied before incidents).

IAC-18.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about lifecycle management of user secrets (storage, rotation, revocation and incident counts) rather than broader access provisioning or monitoring; measurement must focus on credential attributes, rotation and revocation events rather than general access logs.

IAC-19.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of asset-owner access reviews completed within their scheduled interval, the count of user access changes (revocations/modifications) resulting from those reviews, and the number of overdue or missing reviews per review cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires periodic completion by asset owners (coverage/compliance), so a percentage of scheduled reviews completed on time is the most direct auditor metric; counts and rates of changes and trends over time support investigative and trending evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute per review period: (a) number of scheduled access reviews assigned to asset owners, (b) number completed on or before the scheduled due date, (c) list and count of accounts for which access was revoked/modified as a result of the review, and (d) number of overdue or missing reviews. Key fields: review_id, asset_owner_id, scheduled_due_date, review_completion_timestamp, reviewed_account_id, action_taken (revoked/modified/unchanged), and review_notes.
data_signals: access review schedule records (review_id, asset_owner_id, scheduled_due_date)
access review execution logs / completion timestamps
reviewed account identifiers (reviewed_account_id) and entitlement lists
action_taken from review (revoked, modified, retained)
overdue review flags or missed-review entries
audit logs of access changes correlated to review_id
incident/breach events correlated with overdue or missing reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-generated review assignments and completion timestamps with linked action records (revocations/modifications) and owner identifiers showing automated enforcement or logged changes.
Moderate: central ticketed records showing review outcomes but requiring manual reconciliation to access change logs.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or emailed attestations without system timestamps or linked access-change evidence; no linkage to actual account modification logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates asset owners 'review users' access rights at regular intervals,' which is a detective attestation activity designed to find inappropriate access.

IAC-19.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires owner-led, periodic attestation (schedule compliance and review outcomes) rather than continuous access enforcement or provisioning controls; measurement must focus on scheduled review completion, resulting entitlement changes, and overdue reviews.

IAC-20.measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and completeness of access removals and adjustments following triggering events (terminations, role changes, project exits) and the rate of exceptions where access was not revoked/updated within the defined policy timeframe.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control focused on coverage and timeliness (percentage of removals/adjustments completed within policy/SLA). Counts and rates (number of overdue removals, removals per period) and trend metrics supplement by showing volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of triggering events (HR terminations, role changes, project exits) that had corresponding access removals/adjustments executed within the policy SLA, plus the count and list of outstanding (overdue) access entitlements by user and system. Also measure mean time-to-revoke (hours) from event to enforcement and number of exceptions with justification.
data_signals: provisioning/deprovisioning event logs with timestamps (account disable/delete/change)
HR termination and role-change event records with timestamps
access-request and approval ticket timestamps (request/fulfillment records)
IAM policy change logs showing entitlement modification
list of active user accounts with last entitlement change timestamp
overdue exception records or approved deviation tickets
scheduled access review results showing stale/shared accounts
mean time to revoke/access adjustment per triggering event type
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated correlation of HR events to IAM deprovisioning logs showing enforcement within SLA (continuous system logs).
Strong: system-generated timestamps for request→fulfillment with no manual edits.
Weak: manual spreadsheets or one-off screenshots documenting deprovisioning without system logs.
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals without recorded enforcement timestamps or unresolved exception tickets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates removal or adjustment of access rights (preventive action) to avoid unauthorized access and breaches.

IAC-20.differentiation_note: This control specifically measures lifecycle enforcement (timely removal/adjustment of entitlements after triggering events) rather than periodic access review or initial provisioning; its unique need is correlating HR/project events to recorded deprovisioning actions and SLA timeliness.

IAC-21.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and enforcement of secure handling for all secret authentication information: percent of credentials managed in approved secret stores, count of secret exposures (code/repos/plaintext) and the rate of secret-related incidents, and average rotation/replication times versus policy requirements.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires measuring coverage/compliance (percentage of secrets under approved management). Counts (exposures) and rates (incident frequency) are useful secondary measures to detect failures and remediation velocity; trending shows improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of credentials and secret artifacts (user passwords, API keys, certificates, tokens) stored in an approved secrets manager vs. unmanaged storage; count of secret exposures discovered (scans and incidents) per period; timestamps of last rotation per secret and number of secrets exceeding rotation policy; number of accounts without enforced MFA or password policy.
data_signals: inventory of secrets managed in approved secret manager (identifier, owner, last-rotation-timestamp)
results from automated secret-scanning of code repositories and artifact storage (file/path, secret-type, discovery-timestamp)
authentication system logs showing MFA-enabled flag per account
password policy enforcement logs (policy version, enforcement failures, timestamped events)
incident records tagged with 'credential compromise' or 'secret exposure' (incident id, detection time, root cause)
privileged account list with storage method attribute (managed vs unmanaged)
counts of password/credential reset requests tied to suspected compromise
audit logs of access to secrets (who accessed secret, timestamp, source IP)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated secret-manager inventory + automated repository secret-scan results + audit logs showing rotation timestamps and access events.
Moderate: Periodic reports from secret-management tools without continuous access logs.
Weak: Manual spreadsheets or one-off statements that secrets are 'protected' or single annual assessments; absence of automated scan data weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control governs proper use and handling of secret authentication information (preventing misuse/exposure), so it is primarily preventive.

IAC-21.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about handling and protection of authentication secrets (storage, rotation, exposure) rather than broader access provisioning or incident response — measurement must focus on secret inventory/management and exposure detection rather than general access-review counts.

IAC-22.measurement_goal: Measure coverage of interactive password management across active accounts and the effectiveness of password quality enforcement: percent of accounts governed by the password management system, distribution of password-strength scores for newly created/changed passwords, and count/rate of password set attempts rejected by quality checks.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires verifying coverage and policy enforcement: percentage metrics show coverage/compliance (primary), while distributions and counts show strength and enforcement activity (secondary). Score is useful for password-strength measurements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of active user accounts whose authentication is enforced by the interactive password management system (accounts_managed_by_pwd_mgr / total_active_accounts). 2) Distribution of latest password-strength scores (e.g., zxcvbn or entropy) for password create/change events over the audit period. 3) Count and timestamps of password set/change attempts rejected by quality checks with rejection reason codes.
data_signals: password policy configuration settings export (min_length, complexity flags, banned_password_list)
user account inventory with flag indicating managed-by-password-management-system
password creation/change event logs (user_id, timestamp, source, strength_score)
password quality rejection events (timestamp, user_id, reason_code)
latest password strength score per account (entropy or zxcvbn score)
daily count of password set/change attempts vs. rejected attempts
unmanaged account count (accounts not routed through password management system)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous event logs showing every password create/change with strength_score and explicit rejection reason codes plus an exported policy configuration from the password management system.
Moderate: periodic reports generated by the password manager summarizing enforcement and rejected attempts.
Weak: manual attestations or single screenshots of policy settings without supporting event logs or coverage data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control language 'shall ensure quality passwords' and 'shall be interactive' describes proactive enforcement to prevent weak credentials, which is preventive in nature.

IAC-22.differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on interactive password-management enforcement and measurable password-quality outcomes (coverage, strength distributions, rejection events), not on broader access reviews, MFA, or incident detection.

IAC-23.measurement_goal: Measure authorization, usage, and logging of privileged utility program executions: percentage of privileged-utility executions that are performed by authorized accounts with recorded approval and audit trail, and the count of unauthorized or unlogged executions detected.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive — auditors need coverage/ compliance percentages (e.g., percent of executions with approvals/logs). Counts and rates (unauthorized executions, elevation requests) are useful secondary indicators and trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of privileged-utility program executions with associated user ID, timestamp, source host, and correlated approval/authorization record; inventory of privileged utility accounts and their entitlement status; number of executions without matching approval or without audit log entries; retention of execution logs and session recordings per policy.
data_signals: privileged utility execution logs (user id, timestamp, command/utility name, source host)
approved access/authorization records for utility usage (request id, approver, approval timestamp, scope)
privileged utility account inventory (account id, assigned user, entitlement status)
session recording or command audit trail for privileged utility sessions
authentication/MFA logs tied to utility usage events
exception or temporary elevation records with expiry timestamps
log retention timestamps and integrity/hash of audit logs
counts of executions without matching approval or missing audit trail
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, automated execution logs correlated with approval records and session recordings; alerts for executions by accounts not listed in the privileged utility inventory.
Weak: one-off manual approvals, periodic spreadsheets of privileged accounts, or screenshots of logs that cannot be cross-referenced to execution events.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Use of privileged utility programs' indicates restrictions and controls to prevent unauthorized use of high-risk utilities (preventive intent).

IAC-23.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about controlling and auditing execution of privileged utility programs (execution-level authorization and logging) rather than broader privileged account provisioning or general access reviews.

IAO-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of development projects/builds that execute and pass defined security tests before release, the count of security test executions per build (SAST/DAST/functional security tests), and the number and severity of security defects identified and remediated during development versus post-release.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage verification across development work: percentage measures (coverage/compliance) show whether testing 'shall be carried out' for all projects; counts and trends show test execution volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics: (1) percent of projects/releases with evidence of security tests executed prior to deployment (traceable test execution timestamps tied to build IDs); (2) count of security test executions per build by test type (SAST/DAST/interactive); (3) number and severity of security defects opened and closed during development with resolution timestamps compared to deployment time. Include traceability from security requirements to test cases and a signed-off test report or pipeline pass record prior to release.
data_signals: test execution logs with timestamps and build/release ID (showing security tests ran before deployment)
CI/CD pipeline job results indicating security-test stages passed/failed
inventory of security test cases mapped to project security requirements (traceability matrix)
signed test reports or QA/security sign-off records for each release
defect tracker entries labeled as security with created/resolved timestamps and severity
percentage of projects/releases with 'security testing completed' flag or checklist item
list of projects without recorded security tests (uncovered projects)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD pipeline logs showing security test stages executed and green before deployment, plus traceability matrix and signed-off test reports for each release
Moderate: periodic manual test reports with timestamps but lacking automated pipeline evidence
Weak: one-off or post-release penetration test reports without proof that tests were executed during development

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'shall be carried out during development' indicates actions taken proactively to prevent security issues before release, aligning with preventive controls.

IAO-3.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires evidence of security testing performed in-development (pre-release) and traceability to security requirements, unlike sibling controls that may focus on runtime detection or post-deployment remediation; measurement must therefore emphasize pre-deployment test execution and coverage.

IRO-5.measurement_goal: Measure existence, currency, and operational readiness of documented incident response responsibilities, contact lists/escalation procedures, and forensic/remediation procedures — and the percentage of incidents with an assigned owner and documented follow-up within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (presence and coverage of roles/procedures), so percentage metrics (e.g., percent of required procedures present or percent of incidents with assigned owners) show coverage; counts and trends complement to show exercise frequency and assignment volumes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Presence and last-review timestamp of an incident response plan and documented forensic procedures; 2) completeness of an incident contacts/escalation list (roles, phone/email, escalation tiers) and a RACI or responsibilities matrix; 3) percent of incidents with an owner assigned within the policy SLA and percent of post-incident remediation action items closed within required timeframes; 4) records of tabletop/exercise results and associated remediation backlogs.
data_signals: incident response plan document version and last review timestamp
forensic procedures document availability and version
validated incident response contact list with escalation tiers and verification timestamp
RACI / responsibilities matrix mapping roles to activities
incident/ticket system fields: incident created timestamp, owner assignment timestamp, owner role
post-incident action item records: remediation task creation and closure timestamps
tabletop/exercise reports and remediation backlog items
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped contact verification logs, versioned IR and forensic procedure documents, ticketed incident assignments showing owner assigned within SLA, and completed tabletop/exercise reports with tracked remediation closures.
Weak: single ad-hoc emails or spreadsheets, undocumented or expired procedures, offline attestations, or anecdotal evidence without timestamps showing assignment or closure.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on defining responsibilities and procedures to prevent slow or unclear incident response (establishing roles/contacts and procedures), which is preventive in nature.

IRO-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of documented roles, contact/escalation maintenance, and forensic/remediation procedure coverage — unlike sibling controls that might focus on detection or remediation timeliness, this one emphasizes procedure presence, currency, and assignment completeness.

IRO-6.measurement_goal: Measure the timeliness and completeness of reporting information security events: the proportion of reportable events that are notified to required internal and external contacts within the policy/SLA window, plus the currency of the maintained contact lists and notification audit trails.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Reporting effectiveness is best expressed as rates (e.g., rate of incidents reported within SLA); percentages (compliance %) and counts (number of reportable incidents, missed reports) provide complementary evidence; trending shows improvement or regressions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of detected security events classified as 'reportable', timestamp of detection, timestamp of first internal report (ticket/email/notification), timestamp of external/regulatory notification (if applicable), and the last-verified timestamp for required contact entries; compute time-to-report per incident and percentage reported within the policy/SLA.
data_signals: security event / incident detection timestamps (ticket creation or detection alert time)
incident report creation timestamps (internal notification/ticket IDs)
external notification timestamps and recipients (regulatory/partner notifications)
time-to-report metric per incident (minutes/hours between detection and first report)
reportable-incident flag or classification and severity
contact list entries with role, organization, and last verification timestamp
notification delivery/audit logs (email delivery receipts, SMS / pager logs, SIEM/automation notification logs)
policy or SLA definitions for reporting timeframes
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident ticketing with detection and notification timestamps, delivery receipts, and an auditable contact list with recent verification dates.
Weak: ad-hoc manual emails without consistent timestamps, missing delivery receipts, or stale/unknown contact list versions.
Strong: consistent % of reportable incidents meeting SLA over time; Weak: sporadic point-in-time evidence or a single retrospective report.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control title 'Reporting information security events' and associated risks reference 'delays in breach response and reporting', indicating this control is focused on detection-to-notification and therefore primarily detective.

IRO-6.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on notification timeliness, contact-list currency, and audit trails for reporting events — distinct from sibling controls that might measure detection coverage or remediation speed.

IRO-7.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and sources of reported information-security weaknesses, the timeliness of acknowledgement and triage (time-to-acknowledge, time-to-triage), and the percentage of reported weaknesses that are escalated, remediated or closed within defined policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective-style control: auditors need counts of reported weaknesses and rates (acknowledgement, escalation, closure) to demonstrate detection and triage activity; percentages (e.g., closed within policy) and trends show effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of weakness reports per reporting channel and source per period; timestamps for submission, acknowledgement, triage action, escalation and closure to compute time-to-acknowledge and time-to-close; classification/severity assigned and whether the report resulted in remediation or an incident ticket.
data_signals: weakness report submission records (timestamp, reporter id or anonymous flag, reporting channel)
acknowledgement/triage timestamps and triage owner assignments
ticketing/incident IDs created from reports (linkage between report and incident)
report classification/severity labels and root-cause tags (e.g., vulnerability, process gap, remote-site configuration)
remediation or closure timestamps and resolution notes
policy or procedure document indicating reporting process and SLA targets
training/awareness completion records for staff on how to report weaknesses
counts of unacknowledged or overdue reports past policy SLA
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated report intake with immutable timestamps, linked ticket IDs, triage actions logged, and SLA timestamps for acknowledgement/closure
Strong: sustained trend of reports being acknowledged and closed within policy thresholds and documented escalations to incident response
Weak: ad-hoc email submissions without tracked timestamps, no linkage to ticketing or triage records, or only periodic manual summaries

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on 'reporting information security weaknesses', which is primarily a detection and notification activity requiring evidence of reported events and triage.

IRO-7.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about intake, acknowledgement and triage of reported weaknesses (reporting metrics and timeliness), unlike sibling controls that would focus on remediation SLAs, forensic capability, or vulnerability scanning coverage.

IRO-8.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of information security events that receive a documented assessment and decision (classification, escalation or closure) within the required timeframe, plus timing metrics (time-to-assessment, time-to-decision), and the fraction of assessed events that have forensic triage or remediation tasks opened.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires confirmation that events are being assessed and decisions made in a timely manner — a rate (e.g., % of events assessed within target) best shows compliance; counts and trends support volume and longitudinal behavior while distributions show time-to-decision spread.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of information security events ingested per period and, for each event, timestamps for event creation, assessment start and assessment decision; classification/escalation labels; whether forensic triage was initiated; linked remediation/ticket IDs and responder assignment. From these derive: percent of events assessed within policy-defined timeframe, median time-to-decision, number of events escalated, and percent of events with forensic evidence collection.
data_signals: event intake/alert logs with creation timestamp and unique event ID
assessment/triage records with assessor ID, assessment_start and assessment_decision timestamps
event classification labels (e.g., incident, false_positive, security_event)
escalation records and escalation timestamps (who escalated, to which team)
forensic collection logs / evidence collection initiation timestamps
linked remediation ticket IDs and remediation/ticket open timestamps
SLA or policy-defined assessment/decision time thresholds
event closure timestamp and root cause analysis flag/record
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated ingestion and timestamped triage records for every event (event ID mapped to assessment and decision timestamps) and automated linkage to forensic/remediation tickets.
Weak: ad-hoc emails or manual spreadsheet notes without consistent timestamps or missing linkage between events and remediation actions.
Strong: presence of policy-defined time thresholds and machine-recorded SLA compliance rate; Weak: only retrospective incident reports with no per-event timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on assessing events and making decisions (triage/classification/escalation), which is a detection/triage activity rather than a preventive or purely corrective action.

IRO-8.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about timely assessment and decisioning (triage/classification/escalation) of security events, distinct from sibling controls that would measure remediation completion, forensic capability, or assignment of responsibilities; its measurements must capture per-event timestamps and decision records rather than only remediation outcomes.

IRO-9.measurement_goal: Measure how promptly and consistently information security incidents are handled according to the documented incident response procedures: MTTR (detection → containment → recovery), percent of incidents with completed procedure checklists, and presence/currency of required contacts/forensic actions and notifications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a corrective control the most useful primary metric is a rate (e.g., response/remediation velocity, SLA compliance rate). Secondary metrics (procedure adherence percentage, incident counts, and trends over time) demonstrate coverage, frequency and improvement in response processes as required by the documented procedures and associated risks (contacts, forensics, remediation).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each incident capture timestamps for detection, assignment, containment, eradication and recovery to compute MTTR phases; record whether the incident response checklist/playbook version was applied and completed; record forensic evidence collection artifacts and chain-of-custody entries; track contact list version/last-updated and notifications sent to regulators/insurers where applicable. Aggregate these to compute SLA compliance rates, percent checklist completion, and counts of incidents with preserved forensics.
data_signals: incident ticket timestamps: time_detected, time_assigned, time_contained, time_eradiated, time_recovered
incident response checklist/playbook ID and completion flag with completion timestamp
forensic evidence collection logs and chain-of-custody records (artifact IDs, collection timestamps)
incident notification records to internal contacts, regulators or insurer (email/ticket records with timestamps)
incident classification and severity
contact list document/version and last_updated timestamp and owner
remediation action items created with due dates and closure timestamps
post-incident report filed flag and report timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident timelines and workflow events (ticket timestamps across phases) with immutable logs
Strong: completed digital checklists/playbooks tied to the incident record and playbook versioning
Strong: signed chain-of-custody and timestamped forensic artifacts stored in evidence repository
Weak: retrospective free-text incident summaries or emailed notes without timestamps tied to the incident record
Weak: manual, infrequently updated contact lists or lack of formal notification records to regulators/insurers

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires that incidents 'shall be responded to' per procedures — this describes actions taken after incidents occur (corrective).

IRO-9.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about execution of incident response and adherence to documented procedures (timeliness, checklist completion, contact/forensic actions) rather than detection, prevention, or vulnerability remediation, so measurements must capture response timelines and procedural evidence rather than detection coverage.

IRO-10.measurement_goal: Measure completion and timeliness of post-incident reviews (RCA), the rate and percentage of corrective action items from those reviews closed within policy/SLA, and the frequency of repeat incidents attributable to previously identified root causes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Learning-from-incident controls are corrective/improvement-focused: primary rate metrics (e.g., action-item closure rate) show remediation velocity; percentage and count measure coverage and volume of reviews/actions, and trend shows improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of incidents with a completed post-incident review (RCA) and timestamp, count and percentage of RCA-derived action items with assigned owner and completion timestamp, mean time-to-root-cause-identification per incident, number of repeat incidents tied to the same root cause, and records showing updates to project plans/policies or change requests that reference incident lessons.
data_signals: post-incident review / RCA report records with completion timestamp
forensic analysis reports tied to incidents
action-item or remediation tickets created from RCA with owner, due date, status, and completion timestamp
time-to-root-cause-identification per incident
repeat incident occurrences mapped to prior root-cause IDs
change requests or project-plan updates that reference incident RCA or lessons learned
evidence of assigned information security responsibilities and policy updates after incidents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated ticketing records showing RCA creation date, assigned owner, and closure timestamps; forensic reports linked to incident IDs; change control records and updated project plans that explicitly reference lessons learned; measurable reduction in repeat incidents over time.

Weak: ad-hoc email summaries or undocumented meeting notes without timestamps or owners; single one-off postmortem documents with no tracked remediation tickets; no linkage between lessons learned and changes to projects/policies.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on 'learning from information security incidents' and addressing 'vulnerabilities and root causes', which indicate corrective action and remediation processes.

IRO-10.differentiation_note: Unlike detection or response-speed controls, this control requires measurement of post-incident review completion, tracked remediation action items and integration of lessons into project plans and policies; measurement must link RCA artifacts to ticketing and change records (note: control description was not provided, so keywords were drawn from associated risks).

IRO-11.measurement_goal: Measure the presence, completeness and integrity of forensic evidence collected for each security incident: count of incidents with timestamped preserved artifacts, percent of incidents with documented forensic reports/analysis, and time from detection to evidence preservation to demonstrate evidence is collected and usable for investigation/prosecution.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Evidence collection is best evidenced by event counts (e.g., incidents with preserved artifacts) and complemented by percentages/rates of compliance over time to show coverage and improvement; counts show occurrence while percentages show compliance to policy.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each incident record the incident_id, whether evidence was collected (boolean), types of artifacts preserved (system logs, network captures, disk images, application logs), timestamps for each collection action, integrity/hash values for preserved artifacts, presence of a forensic report/root-cause analysis, and time interval from detection to first evidence preservation action. Aggregate these to produce counts and compliance percentages by incident severity.
data_signals: incident_id
evidence_collection_timestamp
evidence_collected_flag (yes/no)
evidence_artifact_type (system_log, network_capture, disk_image, application_log)
artifact_integrity_hash (SHA256)
forensic_report_exists (boolean) / forensic_report_timestamp
root_cause_analysis_report_exists (boolean)
time_to_collect_evidence_minutes
incident_severity / criticality
remediation_record linked to incident
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped exports and preserved artifacts with integrity hashes and documented forensic reports and collection timestamps for each incident
Strong: documented evidence collection steps that are linked to the incident ticket and show preservation prior to remediation
Weak: manual recollections, screenshots, or ad-hoc notes without timestamps/hashes or not linked to a formal incident record
Weak: sporadic/annual forensic reports without per-incident artifact preservation records

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Collection of evidence' and associated risk text about 'forensic procedures' and identifying/prosecuting actors indicate this is a detective/forensic evidence preservation activity performed during incidents.

IRO-11.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on preservation and integrity of forensic artifacts and forensic reporting (evidence collection and usability) rather than detection of incidents or remediation speed; measurement must show per-incident artifact capture and integrity rather than only incident counts or remediation status.

MNT-1.measurement_goal: Measure maintenance coverage and schedule adherence for all managed equipment, and quantify availability and integrity outcomes attributable to maintenance (e.g., maintenance-compliant asset percentage, overdue maintenance count, and equipment-related downtime incidents).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage and compliance rates (percentage of equipment with up-to-date maintenance). Counts (overdue items, maintenance actions) and trends (maintenance adherence over time, downtime trend) complement percentage-based coverage to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track the inventory of equipment with maintenance metadata (last maintenance timestamp, next scheduled maintenance, maintenance status/completed flag) and compute the percentage of equipment compliant with the formal maintenance schedule. Correlate maintenance records with availability and integrity outcomes (equipment-specific uptime/downtime events and integrity check results) and count overdue maintenance items and maintenance-related incidents.
data_signals: equipment inventory records with unique ID and type
maintenance work order records (creation/completion timestamps, technician ID)
last maintenance timestamp and next scheduled maintenance date per asset
maintenance status flag (compliant | overdue | exempt) per asset
equipment downtime/availability logs (start/end timestamps) correlated to asset IDs
integrity verification results or tamper-detection logs for equipment
counts of maintenance-related incidents or failures
maintenance procedure/revision history (document version and effective date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CMDB/asset records with scheduled maintenance and completed work-order logs, integrated uptime logs and integrity checks showing reduced downtime after maintenance events.
Strong: machine-readable maintenance timestamps and unique asset IDs allowing correlation of maintenance to availability/ integrity outcomes.
Weak: annual manual attestations or one-off spreadsheet lists without timestamps or asset identifiers.
Weak: narrative reports that lack correlation between maintenance actions and availability/integrity metrics.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control statement 'Equipment shall be correctly maintained to ensure its continued availability and integrity' describes activities to prevent failures, so it is preventive.

MNT-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measurable maintenance coverage and its effect on equipment availability and integrity (maintenance schedules, work-orders, uptime correlation), unlike sibling controls that might target contacts, threat intelligence, or incident response processes.

MON-5.measurement_goal: Measure log collection coverage, retention and integrity for all critical systems and the rate of logged security-relevant events (and subsequent review/alert actions) to demonstrate event logging is operating and supports identification of information-security issues.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, counts (events ingested, review actions) directly show logging activity; percentages show coverage (systems with logging enabled) and rates/trends show detection activity and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of log events ingested per source and per hour; percentage of inventoryed systems forwarding logs to centralized collection; retention duration per log stream vs. policy; number of integrity verification events (checksums/signature verifications); count and age of security-relevant log alerts and corresponding investigation/closure tickets and SLA adherence.
data_signals: log ingestion timestamps and source host identifiers (per event)
event type / event ID fields for security-relevant events
log forwarding status / agent heartbeat per host
log retention configuration per log stream (retention days)
log integrity verification records (hashes, verification timestamps)
inventory of systems mapped to logging-enabled flag
alert/ticket records created from log analysis (timestamp, owner, status, close timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, automated centralized log ingestion with per-host heartbeats and ingestion timestamps (strong).
Automated integrity verification (hash/signature) for log streams and tamper-evident storage (strong).
High percent coverage of inventoryed systems forwarding logs to central collector (strong).
Sporadic ad-hoc screenshots of logging configuration or annual manual reports only (weak).
Log sources with frequent gaps or missing heartbeats, or retention periods shorter than policy (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Event logging' implies monitoring and detection of events rather than preventive configuration or corrective action.

MON-5.differentiation_note: Focused specifically on measurable logging coverage, retention and integrity (collection and review of events) rather than broader governance or project-planning responsibilities reflected in associated risks.

MON-6.measurement_goal: Report counts of detected tampering or unauthorized access attempts against logging facilities and measure the coverage of protective controls (integrity checks, access restrictions, encryption) across all log repositories; produce the rate of integrity verification failures and the percentage of log stores with enforcement of access controls and immutable storage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective control focused on identifying tampering and unauthorized access events (counts of events and alerts are primary); rates (events per time) and percentages (coverage of protected log stores) help show detection frequency and completeness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count the number of unauthorized-access events and tamper-detection alerts against logging systems (including user/service identity, timestamp, action) and measure the percentage of log repositories with active integrity verification (checksums/hashes), access controls (ACLs/roles), encryption at rest, and immutable or backed-up copies.
data_signals: log storage access audit records (actor, timestamp, action: read/write/delete)
integrity verification records (checksum/hash generation and verification timestamps and results)
SIEM/IDS alerts tagged as 'log tampering' or 'unauthorized access to logging facility'
configuration change events for log repositories (ACL changes, retention policy edits) with actor and timestamp
count of accounts/roles with write/delete privileges on log stores
encryption-at-rest configuration flag per log repository
WORM/immutable storage or backup snapshot metadata and verification records
failed integrity verification or tamper-detection incident tickets with remediation timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated integrity verification logs (checksums with periodic verification) and SIEM alerts are strong evidence.
Centralized access audit records and immutable/WORM storage proving no post-facto modification are strong evidence.
One-off manual checks or single point-in-time screenshots are weak evidence.
Absence of audit trails for ACL/configuration changes or missing verification timestamps weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text focuses on protection 'against tampering and unauthorized access' and the provided control type is 'detective', indicating detection and evidence of tampering/access is required.

MON-6.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets integrity and access protection for logging systems (tamper detection, integrity checks, ACLs and immutable storage) rather than general access control or project-level security planning, so measurement must focus on log-specific integrity and access signals.

MON-7.measurement_goal: Measure completeness and integrity of logs for administrator and operator activity: percent of privileged sessions and privileged commands captured and forwarded to central logging, plus counts of administrative events (authn/authz, session start/stop, privileged commands) and evidence of log retention and tamper protection.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective monitoring control, raw event counts (admin authentication events, privileged command events) are primary to show logged activity; percentages show coverage (what share of privileged activity is logged) and rates/trends show changes over time and log-forwarding/ingestion health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of administrator/operator authentication events, privileged session start/stop events, and privileged command audit records per host/system per day (fields: timestamp, user_id, role, session_id, source_ip, action/command, success/failure), plus log ingestion/forwarding status to central SIEM, log retention metadata (retention period and last retention check), and log integrity/hash or WORM storage indicators.
data_signals: administrator authentication event logs (timestamp, user_id, role)
operator/privileged command audit records (command, session_id, result)
session start/stop events for privileged accounts (timestamp, session_id, source_ip)
central SIEM/log-collector ingestion timestamps and ingestion success/failure flags
log retention policy records and retention timestamps for admin logs
log integrity checksums/hashes and tamper-detection alerts
access-to-logstore audit events (who accessed/modified logs)
log forwarding failure alerts and backlog counts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated ingestion of privileged activity logs into a centralized, immutable store with integrity hashes and detailed fields (user, session, command) and monitored ingestion success metrics.
Weak: periodic manual screenshots or ad-hoc reports of a few admin events, or local logs not forwarded/archived and lacking integrity checks.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on recording administrator and operator activity (logs), which is inherently detective — capturing events for detection and investigation.

MON-7.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on logging of privileged/administrator/operator activity (completeness, forwarding, retention, integrity), unlike sibling monitoring controls that may target system or application metrics more generally.

NET-6.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of teleworking controls by reporting the percentage of remote sessions using approved encrypted channels with MFA, the percentage of remote endpoints that are company-managed and meet endpoint-security baseline, the count of privileged or vendor remote access accounts, and the completion rate of teleworking security training/policy acknowledgement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Teleworking is primarily preventive (coverage/compliance) so percentage metrics (coverage, compliance rate) best show policy adherence; counts and rates are useful as secondary evidence (e.g., number of noncompliant devices, remote access attempts); scores can summarize posture across devices/users.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) percent of remote sessions in the audit window that used approved encrypted tunnels/TLS + MFA, (b) percent of remote endpoints that are company-managed and pass baseline endpoint security checks, (c) count of active remote access accounts flagged as vendor or privileged and their segregation status, and (d) percent of remote workers who have completed teleworking security training and signed policy acknowledgement.
data_signals: VPN/session logs with timestamp, source IP, cipher suite/TLS version, and MFA event flag
MFA enrollment and authentication success/failure logs for remote access
endpoint management system (MDM/EPP) inventory with device ownership tag and compliance status (antivirus/patch/secure config)
list of remote access accounts with attributes: user/vendor flag, privilege level, last use timestamp
teleworking/security policy acknowledgement and training completion records with timestamps
records of encrypted channel usage for remote services (TLS/SSH termination logs) showing in-transit encryption
exceptions/approved deviations registry for remote access or vendor access
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated VPN/session logs showing cipher/TLS version and MFA events combined with MDM/EPP compliance dashboards and up-to-date device inventory; continuous monitoring of vendor remote accounts and recorded approvals.
Weak: periodic manual attestations, policy documents alone, one-off training attendance without verification, or isolated pen-test reports that do not show ongoing session/device telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Teleworking aims to prevent insecure remote access and data breaches by enforcing controls (encryption, endpoint posture, segregation, training), so it functions primarily as a preventive control.

NET-6.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets remote/telework scenarios — measurement must emphasize remote session encryption, endpoint management for remote devices, vendor/segregated remote access, and teleworking training/acknowledgement rather than generic network configuration or vulnerability scanning.

NET-7.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of network management controls that protect information in systems and applications: percent of network devices and segments compliant with approved secure configurations and segmentation policies, percentage of in-transit flows encrypted per application/system, count of authorized third-party/vendor network access connections and overdue access approvals, and count/rate of unauthorized network changes or network-attributable incidents.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: NET-7 is preventive and primarily requires coverage/compliance measurement (e.g., percent of devices/flows meeting encryption and segmentation policies). Counts and rates (e.g., unauthorized change counts, vendor connections) and a configuration/compliance score are useful secondary views to show volume and severity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Queryable metrics showing: 1) percentage of network devices (by type and location) with approved secure configuration baselines applied; 2) percentage of network segments enforcing approved segmentation/ACL rules; 3) percentage of application/system network flows encrypted in transit (TLS/IPSec) and exceptions; 4) inventory and count of vendor/third-party network access accounts with approval and expiry status; 5) count of unauthorized network changes or network-origin security incidents during the period.
data_signals: network device configuration inventory with baseline compliance flag and last configuration timestamp
network segmentation map/segment membership and segment-enforcement status
firewall/ACL rule repository and rule-change audit logs (change author, timestamp, approval)
VPN/remote-access session logs with MFA flag, source IP, and session timestamps
network flow logs / TLS handshake metadata indicating encryption (cipher, protocol) per application/system
vendor/third-party network access list with access approval records and expiry dates
network change requests and approval timestamps from change_management system
incident records attributed to network controls (incident cause code = network misconfiguration or in-transit compromise)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated configuration management and compliance scans (device configs with timestamps) are strong evidence; periodic manual spreadsheets or one-off snapshots are weak.
Centralized firewall/ACL rule repositories with change-audit and approval trails are strong; undocumented local rule changes are weak.
Network flow logs showing TLS/IPSec metadata for the full population of application flows are strong; sample captures or no encryption metadata are weak.
Documented vendor access approvals with expirations and automated deprovisioning evidence are strong; verbal approvals or missing expiry dates are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates networks be 'managed and controlled to protect information', which is a preventive requirement to stop exposures before they occur.

NET-7.differentiation_note: This control focuses broadly on network-level management (segmentation, encryption-in-transit, vendor/remote access and device configuration) rather than on host/application-specific or incident-response controls, so measurements must emphasize network device/configuration coverage, flow encryption, and authorized network access inventories.

NET-8.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network services that conform to the organization's network security baseline (segmentation, permitted ports/protocols, and encryption-in-transit requirements), plus counts of exceptions such as unsegmented services, services using weak/no encryption, and vendor or mobile endpoints with network access lacking approved controls.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (ensuring services meet baseline/configuration requirements), so coverage/compliance percentages are most useful; counts (number of non-compliant services), rates (rate of newly non-compliant services), and trends (compliance over time) are valuable secondary measures to show changes and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of discovered network services mapped to the service inventory that meet the security baseline: (a) segmentation status (belongs to allowed zone), (b) encryption-in-transit configuration (TLS version/cipher and certificate validity), (c) firewall/ACL rule compliance (allowed ports/protocols), and (d) approved vendor/mobile access controls. Include timestamps of last configuration scan and any approval records for vendor access.
data_signals: network service inventory with service identifiers and criticality
configuration scanner output showing TLS versions/ciphers and certificate validity per service
firewall and ACL rule snapshots showing allowed ports/protocols per service and zone
network segmentation mapping / VLAN/subnet assignments
vendor network access records and access approval artifacts (contracts or access tickets)
VPN and remote access session logs correlated to vendor or mobile device accounts
mobile device network configuration profiles and MDM enrollment status
configuration scan timestamps and scan result diffs (last compliant vs current)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated configuration and TLS scans + up-to-date network service inventory and automated correlation showing 100% of services assessed against the baseline.
Strong: firewall/ACL rule exports and segmentation maps with automated drift detection and timestamps.
Weak: one-time manual network diagrams or ad-hoc screenshots without scan timestamps or automated correlation to inventory.
Weak: vendor access described only in contracts without records of actual network accounts, sessions, or approval tickets.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Security of network services' implies preventive configuration and segmentation controls (encryption-in-transit, vendor/mobile access restrictions) to prevent breaches.

NET-8.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets the security posture and configuration of network services (segmentation, encryption-in-transit, vendor/mobile network access) rather than broader network monitoring or incident response, so measurements must focus on service-level configuration compliance and approved access records.

NET-9.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information service/user/system groups that are placed in approved network zones and the count and rate of network flows or rules that allow cross-zone communication outside approved exceptions; show documented exceptions and approvals for any non-segregated mappings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage/placement of groups into segregated network zones (coverage % is primary). Counts of cross-zone rules and rate of unauthorized flows are useful secondary measures to detect gaps and exceptions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets/users/information services mapped to approved network zones versus total in-scope, number of firewall/ACL or security-group rules permitting cross-zone traffic, number of documented and approved exceptions, and counts of observed cross-zone flows in network telemetry within a reporting window.
data_signals: network zone inventory (zone IDs, purpose, owner)
asset-to-zone mapping records (asset id, service, user-group, assigned zone)
firewall/ACL/security-group rule sets (source zone, destination zone, ports, allow/deny, rule id, last modified)
network flow telemetry (NetFlow/IPFIX) with source zone and destination zone labels and timestamps
microsegmentation policy definitions (host labels, policies applied)
documented segmentation exceptions and approval records (request id, approver, justification, expiry)
change tickets for segmentation/configuration changes (ticket id, before/after rule, author, timestamp)
