audit of VLAN/subnet assignments and misconfigured hosts (host id, vlan/subnet, expected zone)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous network flow telemetry correlated with asset-to-zone mappings and automated rule audits showing zero unauthorized cross-zone allow rules and only approved exceptions.
Strong: automated nightly reconciliation reporting percent coverage of assets assigned to zones and any drift flagged with tickets.
Weak: one-off network diagrams or manual statements without firewall/ACL exports, flow logs, or documented exceptions.
Weak: snapshots of zone mappings without change-history or telemetry to demonstrate enforcement over time.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text uses mandatory language ('shall be segregated') directing configuration of network segmentation to prevent unauthorized access, indicating a preventive control.

NET-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on network-level segregation/segmentation (zone assignment, firewall/ACL enforcement and telemetry of cross-zone flows) rather than logical access, vendor controls, or encryption — measurement must emphasize zone mappings, rule-sets and continuous flow telemetry. If other domain controls address access reviews or encryption, this entry is unique because it requires network topology, ACLs and flow evidence.

OPS-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required operating procedures that are documented, published, and accessible to all roles/users who require them, plus the count of procedures lacking publication, ownership, or access for required users.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and availability; auditors want percent coverage (what share of required procedures are documented and accessible). Counts (number missing) and trends (documents added/updated over time) are useful complements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of required operating procedures that exist in the official document repository, have an assigned owner and last-reviewed timestamp, and are published with access rights granting all users/roles that need them; plus a count/list of procedures without publication, missing owners, or with no access for required roles. Also measure time-since-last-review distribution per procedure to confirm currency.
data_signals: official procedure inventory records (document_id, title, owner, status/published flag)
document metadata: creation_date, last_review_date, version
role-to-document mapping (which roles/users need each procedure)
repository ACLs or publication settings showing which users/roles have access
document access logs (user_id, timestamp, action=view/download)
user acknowledgement/completion records for procedure read/acknowledgement (user_id, document_id, timestamp)
count/list of required procedures flagged as not published or missing owner
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative document inventory with published flag, ACLs, access logs showing intended users accessed the doc, and recent 'last_review_date' timestamps.
Weak: one-off emailed procedures, undated files on shared drives, or manual attestations without access logs or repository metadata.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'shall be documented and made available' is a preventive requirement to avoid human error and availability incidents by ensuring procedures exist and are accessible.

OPS-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurable documentation coverage and accessibility for users (documents, ownership, publication and access evidence), unlike sibling operational controls that may measure technical logging, patching, or incident detection — its unique need is proof of documented procedures and user availability/acknowledgement.

OPS-3.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and currency of documented operational plans and controls across services/projects (percentage of processes with approved procedures and assigned owners), and detect operational deviations (counts of incidents, log-integrity failures, capacity events, and project plans missing security requirements) that indicate planning or control breakdowns.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, the immediate need is to detect and enumerate operational deficiencies (counts of incidents, missing plans, failed log integrity checks). Percentages (coverage) and trends over time are useful secondaries to show improvement and coverage.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of operational processes/services with a current, approved operational procedure and assigned owner; count of projects that include documented information security requirements in their project plans; counts of incidents attributable to planning/control gaps (human error, missing responsibilities, capacity), counts of log-integrity or logging failures; backup/restore test success counts and capacity threshold breach events. Group and filter by environment (production vs non-production) and criticality.
data_signals: inventory of operational procedures with approval timestamps
project plan records indicating inclusion (or absence) of information security requirements
RACI / role-responsibility registry entries mapping security responsibilities
incident reports tagged as 'human error', 'planning gap', or 'operational impact'
log integrity/checksum verification failures and log corruption alerts
backup and restore test results (timestamps, success/failure, test scope)
capacity planning reports and capacity-threshold breach events
change request approvals and scheduled vs actual execution timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated inventories and timestamped approvals (strong) vs. one-off manual signoff documents (weak)
Continuous logging with integrity verification and immutable storage (strong) vs. periodic exportable logs with missing checksums (weak)
Structured project plan fields showing security requirements (strong) vs. ad-hoc textual notes in project documents (weak)
Regular backup/restore test logs showing repeated success (strong) vs. single successful backup with no restore test (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels the control as 'detective' and the measurement emphasis is on detecting deviations and incidents (counts of incidents, log failures, missing plans).

OPS-3.differentiation_note: This control emphasizes operational planning completeness and the detection of planning/control failures (missing procedures, undefined responsibilities, logging integrity and capacity events) rather than technical vulnerability scanning or access-control enforcement; measurement must focus on documentation coverage, assignment of responsibilities, and counts of operational anomalies.

PES-6.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of the physical security perimeter: the percentage of perimeter access points protected by functioning access controls (badge/CID/CCTV), the count and rate of unauthorized/forced-entry events, and the operational availability of environmental and power controls that protect perimeter equipment.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PES-6 is primarily a preventive perimeter control where coverage and functioning rate (percent of doors/cameras/alarms operational) matter most; counts of incidents and trending of incidents/uptime are useful secondary signals to show frequency and direction of change.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of perimeter access points (doors, gates, loading bays) with active, monitored access controls (badge readers/CCTV/alarm) and percentage of those devices reporting healthy status; counts of unauthorized access attempts (badge denied, forced-open, tailgating alarms) and counts/times of environmental/power failures affecting perimeter equipment; mean time to acknowledge and remediate physical security alarms.
data_signals: inventory of perimeter access points with assigned control type (badge reader, manned gate, turnstile)
badge reader event logs (timestamp, credential id, success/fail, door id)
forced-entry/tamper/door-held-open alarm events (timestamp, door id, alarm type)
CCTV camera operational status and recording timestamps for perimeter cameras
visitor / escort logs and physical access exceptions
environmental sensor logs (temperature, smoke, water) for perimeter facilities
UPS/generator status and maintenance/test logs for perimeter infrastructure
physical security incident reports and remediation timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous machine-generated logs (badge events, alarms, CCTV metadata) correlated with an up-to-date inventory showing full device coverage and recent health checks; scheduled UPS/generator test records and automated sensor telemetry.
Weak: occasional manual visitor books, ad-hoc incident reports, or annual attestations without contemporaneous machine logs or device health metrics.

control_type_classification.reasoning: A perimeter control aims to prevent unauthorized physical access and environmental impacts (phrases: 'Physical security perimeter', risk categories 'Access control, Physical and environmental security').

PES-6.differentiation_note: This control focuses on physical perimeter device coverage and operational availability (doors, cameras, alarms, environmental/power systems), unlike sibling controls that may address internal access reviews or procedural responsibilities; measurement must emphasize device inventory, continuous health/status telemetry, and unauthorized-entry event counts.

PES-7.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of physical entry controls by reporting (a) percentage of designated entry points protected by functioning access-control mechanisms and CCTV, (b) count and rate of unauthorized/forced-entry events, and (c) mean time to remediate physical control failures (e.g., locks, sensors, readers).
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Physical entry controls are primarily preventive so coverage and configuration compliance (percent of entry points with functioning controls) is the most important indicator; counts of unauthorized events and rates of device failures are useful secondary metrics to show detection and operational issues.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percent of all defined entry points covered by electronic access control or verified physical barriers and CCTV that are operational (device health OK), the weekly/monthly count of unauthorized access or forced-entry incidents, and the mean time between failure and remediation for access devices and locks. Include per-door identifiers and timestamps so auditors can map events to specific control points.
data_signals: door access control event logs (badge id, door id, timestamp, success/failure)
unauthorized access / forced-entry alarm incidents (timestamp, door id, incident type)
visitor sign-in records with ID verification timestamp and escort indicator
CCTV recording availability and retention logs for entry points (file timestamps, continuity)
access control device uptime/health metrics and maintenance records (device id, last serviced, status)
access revocation timestamps for terminated/changed personnel (user id, revocation timestamp)
physical security incident reports describing breaches or equipment failures (incident id, description, remediation actions)
open/outage duration for failed physical entry devices (start timestamp, end timestamp, root cause)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated door and badge event logs correlated with CCTV footage and device health metrics are strong evidence.
High device uptime and short mean time-to-remediate for failed locks/readers strengthens evidence.
Periodic manual visitor log sheets or ad-hoc incident narratives without correlated automated logs are weak evidence.
Sparse or missing device health/maintenance records indicate weak control operation and increase risk of equipment failures leading to unavailability.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (Physical entry controls) is intended to prevent unauthorized physical access and equipment failures that could cause breaches, which is characteristic of preventive controls.

PES-7.differentiation_note: Unlike logical access controls, this control requires physical-device health and on-site evidence (device uptime, CCTV availability, visitor logs, forced-entry alarms) rather than only user-account or authentication metrics; measurement must include per-door device identifiers and maintenance/remediation timestamps.

PES-8.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of offices, rooms, and facilities that have an approved physical security design and evidence of implemented controls (access control, monitoring, environmental protections), plus counts/rates of physical security incidents (unauthorized access, equipment failure) to demonstrate the controls are applied and effective.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the primary need is to show coverage/implementation (coverage % of sites with design+implementation). Secondary count/rate/trend metrics capture incident volumes, device uptime and remediation trends which validate ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of sites (offices/rooms/facilities) with an approved physical security design document and an implementation checklist signed/completed; count and rate of unauthorized access or breach incidents per site; device uptime/health for physical controls (badge readers, CCTV, environmental sensors) and number of outstanding physical-security remediation items per site.
data_signals: site physical security design documents and approval timestamps
facility implementation checklist records (per-site control list with completion status)
badge swipe/access control logs (user_id, door_id, timestamp, access result)
visitor sign-in / access exception records
CCTV coverage maps and camera uptime/health logs
environmental monitoring logs (power outage events, temperature/humidity alerts, UPS/test results)
physical security incident reports (unauthorized entry, breach, equipment failure) with timestamps and remediation status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated continuous logs (badge swipes, CCTV uptime, environmental sensors) correlated with up-to-date site design docs and completed implementation checklists; periodic signed inspection/audit reports and timely remediation closures.
Weak: single point-in-time photos or annual attestations without device logs, or undocumented manual claims of controls in place.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires that physical security be 'designed and applied', which is a preventive requirement to stop unauthorized access and reduce environmental risks.

PES-8.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the existence and application of physical security design and implementation across sites (coverage and device health), rather than detection/response metrics or cyber vulnerability scanning used by sibling controls; physical-device uptime and per-site implementation checklists are unique measurement needs. (Note: 'physical security' is not a direct focus_area token, so 'access_control' and 'governance_risk_compliance' were chosen as closest matches.)

PES-9.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational effectiveness of physical and environmental protections for critical systems: percent of critical sites/assets with required environmental controls and access protections, count and rate of environmental or external-threat incidents (e.g., temperature/water/power events, physical breaches), and uptime/availability of redundant environmental infrastructure versus policy thresholds.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage verification (coverage % / compliance %) as the main proof; counts and rates of incidents and trending of environmental telemetry provide complementary detective evidence of failures and recovery over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A concrete measurement should show coverage of required protections per critical asset/site (percentage of critical assets with functioning environmental controls and physical access controls), plus counts and timestamps of environmental/physical incidents and the availability metrics for redundant systems (UPS/generator uptime and successful test records). Include remediation / DR test outcomes (RTO/RPO achieved) and incident resolution times to show operational effectiveness.
data_signals: inventory of critical sites/data centers with documented physical controls (perimeter, locks, mantraps) and environmental controls
environmental sensor telemetry (temperature, humidity, water-leak) with timestamps and alert thresholds
CCTV/surveillance coverage records and physical access logs (entry/exit events) for protected areas
UPS and generator test logs, battery health metrics, and runtime records
fire suppression test certificates and maintenance/inspection records
incident/issue tickets for equipment failures or physical breaches with timestamps, cause, and impact
disaster recovery / business continuity test results showing RTO/RPO achieved
maintenance and corrective action records for environmental/physical infrastructure
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous environmental sensor telemetry and automated alert logs correlated to asset inventory are strong evidence.
Regularly scheduled UPS/generator and fire-suppression test logs with pass/fail and date are medium-strength evidence.
A single annual inspection report or undocumented manual checks are weak evidence; lack of correlation between incidents and sensor/maintenance logs weakens confidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Protecting against external and environmental threats' indicates measures designed to prevent breaches, unavailability and equipment failures (preventive focus).

PES-9.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical and environmental protective measures (environmental sensors, power redundancy, physical access controls and DR tests) rather than logical/technical controls such as network or application hardening; selection of cloud_and_infrastructure_security and access_control is an approximation because a dedicated 'physical security' focus area is not in the provided list.

PES-10.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of identified secure areas that have approved, current working procedures applied, plus counts/rates of secure-area access events that are unauthorized or non-compliant with those procedures.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control about coverage and application of procedures — auditors need coverage percentages (what proportion of secure areas have procedures applied). Counts/rates of unauthorized access and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show whether procedures are effective.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of secure areas with an approved, versioned procedure document and a recorded recent application/compliance check (e.g., audit) within policy-defined timeframe; 2) Count and rate of physical access events to secure areas that are unauthorized or flagged as non-compliant; 3) Count and duration of equipment/environment alerts or failures in secure areas tied to procedural response records.
data_signals: secure_area_inventory (area id, classification, coordinates)
procedure_documentation records (procedure id, version, approval timestamp, effective date)
secure_area_audit/compliance records (audit timestamp, auditor, findings, remediation status)
physical_access_log entries (badge_id, person_id, timestamp, door_id, authorization_flag)
visitor_log entries and escort records (visitor_id, host, time-in/time-out)
CCTV/event correlator evidence for secure area entries (camera_id, event_timestamp, matched_badge_id)
environmental_monitoring alerts for secure areas (power_failure, temperature, humidity, timestamp)
maintenance and incident records for equipment failures in secure areas (incident_id, start/end, impact)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated physical access logs (badge events) correlated with procedure audit records and CCTV footage showing authorization matches; up-to-date, versioned procedure documents and recent audit/compliance checks within policy timeframe.
Strong: low and declining rate of unauthorized entry events after procedures applied, and prompt logged procedural responses to environmental/equipment alerts.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots of procedures, infrequent or no audit records, manual visitor sign-in sheets without correlation to access logs, or anecdotal attestations without timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates that "procedures ... shall be designed and applied," which is a preventive requirement to stop unauthorized access and equipment-related impacts before they occur.

PES-10.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of documented procedure coverage and application for physical secure areas (procedural existence, versioning, and compliance audits), rather than technical access revocation or incident investigation metrics; its unique need is proof of procedural coverage and applied audits for physical locations.

PES-11.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of physical controls for delivery and loading areas: percentage of loading/delivery points with access controls (gates/locks/badge readers/CCTV), count and rate of unauthorized access or security incidents originating at loading areas, and percentage of deliveries logged/authorised per policy.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive physical-access control: auditors need coverage/percentage metrics (e.g., % of loading bays with badge control/CCTV). Counts and rates (unauthorized access events, incident counts) are useful secondary metrics to detect failures and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of delivery/loading points instrumented with approved physical controls (badge readers, locked gates, CCTV with retention) vs total registered loading points; (2) count and rate of access attempts to loading areas (authorized vs denied) per period; (3) count of security incidents and breaches tied to delivery/loading areas and number of deliveries recorded in delivery registers versus expected scheduled deliveries.
data_signals: inventory of loading/delivery points with attributes (location id, control types present: badge_reader, gate, CCTV)
badge reader access logs for loading areas (user/device id, timestamp, granted/denied)
CCTV coverage map and video retention logs for loading areas (camera id, coverage area, retention period)
visitor/delivery register and delivery manifests with timestamps and courier identity
incident/ security event reports referencing 'loading', 'delivery', or specific loading bay ids
maintenance and test records for physical barriers and access control devices (last test, status)
security exception/permit records for unscheduled access to loading areas
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated badge/CCTV logs correlated with incident reports and an up-to-date inventory showing ≥ policy threshold coverage; retention of raw logs for the audit period.
Weak: one-time photos or checklist attestations without timestamped logs, or manual register entries without corroborating access logs.
Strong: maintenance/test records showing operational status of gates/readers and timely remediations for faults; Weak: absent or infrequent maintenance evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (delivery and loading areas) targets physical prevention of unauthorized access and damage by securing access points and monitoring entry, so it is primarily preventive.

PES-11.differentiation_note: Unlike controls focused on IT equipment or procedures, this control specifically requires measurement of physical access points (loading bays) and physical-device coverage (badge readers, gates, CCTV) and delivery logging — its unique measurement need is correlating physical access/device logs with delivery manifests and incident reports.

PES-12.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical equipment that is located in approved, secure sites with required environmental protections and restricted physical access, plus counts and rates of site-related incidents (environmental alerts, unauthorized access) and maintenance/inspection compliance against policy.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage and compliance measurements (percentage of equipment meeting siting/protection requirements); counts and rates of incidents and trends over time are useful secondary indicators of protection effectiveness and failures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the share of critical assets that are registered to approved secure sites and are covered by environmental sensors (temperature/humidity/water), physical access controls, and UPS/fire-suppression maintenance; report counts of environmental threshold breaches, unauthorized access attempts to equipment rooms, and percent of scheduled inspections/tests completed on time.
data_signals: asset location registry with site classification (approved/temporary/unprotected)
physical access control logs (card swipes/door events) correlated to equipment room IDs
CCTV/video retention index for equipment areas
environmental sensor logs (temperature, humidity, water-leak) with timestamps and threshold-exceedance events
UPS/backup power test logs and measured backup uptime percentages
fire suppression maintenance and test records with dates and outcomes
equipment inspection and mounting/clearance inspection records (dates, findings, remediation)
unauthorized access incident reports and equipment outage incident records
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuous environmental sensor logs and access-event logs tied to asset IDs are strong evidence; periodic manual inspection reports without correlated logs are weaker.
Complete asset-to-site mapping and on-time maintenance/test records increase evidence strength; missing location metadata or ad-hoc/temporary siting without approvals weakens it.
Correlated evidence (e.g., access log + CCTV + incident report timestamp alignment) is stronger than a single source (e.g., CCTV only).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Equipment siting and protection' implies preventive measures to avoid breaches, equipment failures, and unavailability by securing locations and environmental controls.

PES-12.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical siting, environmental protections and access restrictions for equipment (site-level coverage and environmental sensor/maintenance metrics), differing from sibling controls that may cover logical access, incident response, or configuration management; note: control description was empty, so keywords and signals are derived from associated risk texts.

PES-13.measurement_goal: Measure whether supporting utilities (power, cooling and other facility services) maintain continuous service for critical systems by tracking redundancy coverage, automated sensor/telemetry alerts, frequency and results of maintenance/tests, and utility-related incident frequency and mean time to restore.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires verifying coverage and compliance (percent of critical assets with redundant utilities, percent of successful scheduled tests). Counts and trends (outage counts, MTTR trend) are useful secondary views for incidents and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent coverage of critical assets by redundant utility designs (UPS, generator, dual feed, N+1 cooling), percent of scheduled UPS/generator/environmental maintenance/tests completed on time, count and duration of utility-related outages affecting critical systems, and MTTR for utility-caused incidents. Include asset-to-location mapping so outage events can be correlated to impacted critical company data/systems.
data_signals: UPS/generator test logs with timestamps and pass/fail results
power outage event logs (start/end timestamps) correlated to affected asset IDs
environmental sensor telemetry (temperature, humidity) and threshold alert records with timestamps
maintenance schedules and completion records for power/cooling equipment
incident/ticket records tagged for 'equipment failure' or 'utility' with root cause and MTTR
redundancy and infrastructure configuration records (dual feed, N+1, UPS capacity) per location
asset criticality mapping to physical location (critical company data/systems)
scheduled test frequency policy or policy_threshold for utilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous telemetry (sensor and power event logs) plus automated UPS/generator test logs and documented maintenance completions tied to asset IDs.
Moderate: periodic (monthly) consolidated reports showing test results and outage summaries.
Weak: one-off manual inspection reports or annual statements without timestamped test logs or correlated outage-to-asset evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on ensuring utilities remain available and reliable to prevent equipment failures and unavailability referenced in the associated risks.

PES-13.differentiation_note: Unlike other physical-security controls focused on access or logical protections, PES-13 specifically targets utility infrastructure (power/cooling/utility telemetry and maintenance) so measurements must include telemetry, redundancy configuration, and generator/UPS test logs rather than access logs or policy documents.

PES-14.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of network and equipment cabling that is physically secured (locked racks/enclosures, tamper-evident seals, restricted access) and track unauthorized cable access or cable-related outages and unauthorized changes over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control: auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of cabling secured) to show compliance; counts of incidents (unauthorized access, outages) and trend metrics show frequency and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) The percentage of cabling assets (by asset ID or location) located in secured enclosures or conduits and the percentage covered by tamper-evident measures as of the latest inspection; 2) the count of unauthorized cable access events or cable-related outages and the count of cabling changes performed with/without approved change requests over the reporting period. Measurements should be linkable to asset inventory, inspection/checklist records, access logs and change tickets.
data_signals: cable_inventory records with asset ID, cable route and physical location
physical inspection checklist entries with timestamp, inspector ID and pass/fail for 'secured cabling' items
locked_rack / enclosure status records (lock serial / badge access records) and timestamps
tamper_seal verification logs or photographed inspection evidence
badge access logs and door entry events for communications rooms (timestamps, user IDs)
incident/maintenance tickets tagged 'cable tamper' or 'cable outage' with timestamps and resolution details
change_management approvals / change requests for cabling work (requestor, approver, timestamps)
CCTV footage metadata or retained snapshots for cable routing areas (timestamps, camera IDs)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: ongoing inspection checklists, tamper-seal logs and automated door/badge access logs correlated to cable locations and photographed evidence; continuous CCTV metadata available for incidents.
Weak: one-time photos or a single annual report without inspection timestamps, lack of link between cable inventory and physical access logs, or manual attestations without change-ticket correlation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control 'Cabling security' is intended to prevent unauthorized access or damage to cabling that could lead to breaches or unavailability, so it functions primarily as a preventive control.

PES-14.differentiation_note: Unlike broader physical security controls that focus on facilities or environmental systems, this control requires measurements tied to cable inventory, tamper-evidence, physical access to cabling routes and change-ticket correlation — operational inspection and asset-linkage metrics rather than solely environmental sensor data.

PES-15.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of workspaces and devices that comply with the clear desk and clear screen policy (e.g., no sensitive documents left, screens locked/auto-lock enabled) and track the count and trend of policy violations found during inspections and incidents over time.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive policy that is best evidenced by coverage/compliance rates (percentage of desks/devices compliant); counts of violations and trending show frequency and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of inspected desks and devices compliant with the clear desk/clear screen policy per inspection cycle (inspections_passed / inspections_total), plus counts of violations (documents left, unlocked screens) and timestamps of remediation. Include device-level auto-lock/screen-lock configuration presence and employee policy attestations.
data_signals: workplace inspection logs (inspection_id, inspector_id, timestamp, location, compliance_pass/fail, violation_types)
count of violations found per inspection (sensitive_document_left, unlocked_workstation, unattended_media)
device auto-lock/screensaver configuration reports (device_id, user, auto_lock_enabled, timeout_seconds)
remediation/closure timestamps for reported violations (violation_id, reported_at, remediated_at)
employee policy attestation records (user_id, attestation_date)
incident/near-miss reports referencing exposed physical documents or unlocked screens (incident_id, description, timestamp)
after-hours access logs or badge entries correlated with unlocked workstation observations (badge_event_ts, user_id, location)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: regular automated device configuration inventory showing auto-lock enabled and timestamped, combined with systematic inspection logs and remediation timestamps.
Strong: time-series of inspection pass rates showing sustained high compliance and declining violation counts.
Weak: a single annual attestation or one-off manual photo without inspection metadata or remediation evidence.
Weak: anecdotal reports of compliance without dated inspection logs or device configuration records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is a policy designed to prevent unauthorized disclosure/exposure (clear desk/clear screen), so it is primarily preventive.

PES-15.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical workspace and endpoint screen-lock compliance (inspections and device configuration) rather than logical access controls or incident handling, requiring physical inspection records and device auto-lock evidence.

PRI-18.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of controls that ensure PII is collected and processed with a documented legal basis (including captured consent), that contracts and vendor agreements contain required privacy clauses for international transfers, that DPIAs and record-keeping are completed, and that SARs/deletion requests and privacy training meet policy timeframes and completion targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: PRI-18 is primarily preventive (assurance that PII processing and contractual/privacy controls are in place), so coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., % of processing activities with documented legal basis, % of contracts with required clauses) are most useful; counts and rates (SAR counts, overdue counts) plus trends support operational monitoring.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics an auditor would expect: percentage of PII processing activities with a recorded legal basis and captured consent (by processing activity), percentage of customer/vendor contracts that include required privacy/international transfer clauses, count and percentage of SARs and deletion requests completed within policy timeframes, DPIA completion rate for scoped projects, and training completion percentage for personnel responsible for PII.
data_signals: consent records with data subject identifier, timestamp and scope of consent
registry of processing activities entries showing documented legal basis per activity
contract repository metadata indicating presence/absence of privacy/international transfer clauses
vendor contract register with privacy controls flag and execution date
DPIA register entries and completion status per project
SAR intake timestamp, completion timestamp, outcome and SLA compliance flag
training assignment and completion records for privacy/PII modules
record-keeping/retention logs showing PII retention status and audit trail
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: searchable, timestamped machine-readable records (consent logs, ROPA/processing registry, contract repository flags, DPIA register) and automated SAR workflow timestamps showing SLA compliance.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets, unsigned contract drafts, or verbal attestations without timestamps (evidence lacks auditable trail).
Strong: training LMS records with completion proof and assessment scores vs. weak: attendance lists without verification.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on protecting PII through processes, contracts, DPIAs, record-keeping and training — activities that prevent privacy violations rather than merely detect or correct them.

PRM-4.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active and completed projects that include documented information security considerations (security requirements, risk identification and treatment, and assigned security responsibilities) in their project plans and that have a recorded security review or sign-off.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors want coverage metrics (percentage of projects with security addressed). Counts of projects with/without security evidence and trends over time help demonstrate sustained compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of projects (active and closed within the audit window) whose project plan or charter contains an information security section, has at least one security-related risk recorded and treated in the risk register, and has an assigned information-security responsibility and documented security review/sign-off. Also capture counts of projects missing any of these items and timestamps for security reviews.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The text 'Information security shall be addressed in project management' indicates integration and prevention of security issues through project planning and assignment of responsibilities.

PRM-4.differentiation_note: This control is focused on integration of information security into project documentation and governance (project-level coverage and role assignment), unlike sibling controls that might address operational technical controls or incident response metrics.

PRM-5.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and completeness of information security objectives by quantifying the percentage of objectives that have documented implementation plans with assigned owners, target dates, and measurable KPIs, and track the count of objectives lacking owners, plans, or overdue milestones.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive planning control: auditors need percent coverage/compliance (e.g., % objectives with plans/owners). Counts (objectives missing artifacts) and trending (improvement over time) are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of documented information security objectives that have a linked implementation plan, an assigned owner, a target date or milestone, and one or more measurable KPIs; count of objectives without owners, without plans, or with overdue milestones; records of periodic reviews/updates for each objective.

count of objectives flagged as overdue or without assigned owner

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing objectives and planning to achieve them — a proactive (preventive) activity as indicated by 'planning to achieve them'.

PRM-5.differentiation_note: Unlike operational or incident-specific controls, this control focuses on governance-level measurable planning artifacts (objectives, owners, KPIs, review cadence) rather than detection or remediation events.

PRM-6.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of active and recent projects whose project plans explicitly address information security (security requirements and risk assessments) and have defined, allocated information security responsibilities; report the count of projects missing these elements and the time-to-assignment for security roles per project.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily preventive — auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of projects meeting the requirement). Counts of non‑compliant projects and trends over reporting periods are useful secondary metrics to show scale and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of projects with a documented information security section or checklist in the project plan, the presence of a named/assigned security role in the project RACI, and an associated risk assessment record. responsibility_assignment_timestamp vs project_start_date
projects_missing_security_section_count (per reporting period)
project_audit_or_review findings noting absent security responsibilities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated project management metadata showing a security_section flag, explicit RACI entries with named security owner, and recorded risk assessment file IDs dated before or at project initiation.
Strong: resource allocation records (FTE/budget) linked to project tasks for security work.
Weak: ad-hoc emails or informal chat notes claiming responsibility without RACI/project metadata.
Weak: a single annual manual review or post-facto checklist instead of project-level artifacts and timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on ensuring security is addressed in project planning and that responsibilities are defined/allocated — actions that prevent security gaps before they occur.

PRM-6.differentiation_note: Unlike runtime controls, this control requires measurement tied to project-management artifacts (project plans, RACI, resource allocations) rather than system logs or technical detections; therefore metrics must map to project coverage and assignment timestamps.

PRM-7.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active projects whose project plans explicitly include information security requirements and have defined/allocated information security responsibilities, and count projects missing these items so auditors can verify that security is communicated and ownership assigned.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive communication control: auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of projects with security requirements and assigned responsibilities). Counts of noncompliant projects and trends over time supplement the coverage percentage to show remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) percentage of active project plans that include an explicit 'information security' section or checklist item; (b) percentage of active projects with an assigned information security owner (RACI entry); and (c) count of projects missing either required security content or assigned responsibilities. Include timestamps to show when requirements/assignments were added and any sign-off evidence.

project communications (meeting minutes or change approvals) that reference security decisions or allocations

affinity_keywords: risks are not identified and treated

control_type_classification.reasoning: The associated risks describe failure to address security in project plans and undefined responsibilities — communication of requirements and assignment of roles prevents those failures.

PRM-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets embedding and communicating information security requirements and role allocation in project planning documents (coverage and ownership), unlike sibling controls that might focus on technical safeguards or incident processes.

RSK-4.measurement_goal: Measure the number and coverage of formal information security risk assessments performed against projects/assets, and the proportion of identified risks that have documented treatment plans and owners (with timestamps) to demonstrate the risk assessment process is defined and applied.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control the most direct evidence is counts (how many risk assessments performed, how many risks identified). Percentages (coverage of projects with assessments, percent of risks treated) and trends over time show ongoing application and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risk assessment records linked to project IDs/assets (with assessment_date and assessor_id), percent of active projects/assets with a completed risk assessment in the required lifecycle stage, count and status of risk_register entries created by those assessments, and counts/timestamps of treatment actions (owner, due_date, completion_date). Also capture existence/version/approval date of the risk assessment process document.
data_signals: risk assessment procedure document (version, approval_date)
risk_assessment records (project_id or asset_id, assessment_date, assessor_id)
project plan metadata with risk_assessment_attached flag
risk_register entries (risk_id, description, likelihood, impact, identified_date, status)
treatment action records (action_id, owner, due_date, completion_date)
coverage metric: number of projects/assets with completed assessment vs. total active projects/assets
overdue treatment count (treatment where completion_date > due_date or status = open past SLA)
assessment scope field indicating remote/mobile/worksite considerations (e.g., 'remote work site', 'mobile device')
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized risk register and risk_assessment records with project linkage, timestamps, assessor IDs and automated coverage dashboards showing % of projects assessed over time.
Strong: documented treatment actions with owners and completion timestamps recorded in the same system as the risk register.
Weak: only a standalone policy document without evidence of assessments, or ad-hoc spreadsheets/one-off attestations lacking timestamps or project linkage.
Weak: manual email approvals or unsigned documents that don't show consistent application across projects.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires defining and applying a 'risk assessment process' which is primarily aimed at identifying (detecting) information security risks across projects and assets.

RSK-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the existence and application of a risk assessment process across projects/assets (process coverage, assessments performed, risks identified and treated) rather than on technical remediation or incident response metrics; measurement must link assessments to project plans and treatment records.

RSK-5.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and application of a documented information security risk treatment process by tracking the percent of identified information security risks that have an assigned treatment plan, the percent of projects that include documented security risk assessments/treatment, and the count/age of untreated or overdue treatment actions (including mobile device risks).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires measuring coverage and compliance (percent of risks with treatment plans, percent of projects including security risk treatment); counts and trends supplement by showing volume and remediation velocity over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risks recorded in the risk register, number and percentage of those risks with a documented treatment plan and assigned owner, timestamps for risk identification and treatment plan creation, treatment plan status and completion timestamps, presence of security risk assessment/treatment entries in project plans, and entries covering mobile device risk assessments.
data_signals: risk register entries (risk id, description, identification_timestamp)

evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated/routinized risk register with timestamps, owner assignments, status updates and linked treatment artifacts (e.g., mitigation tasks) showing ongoing application of the process

Weak: one-off policy document or ad-hoc spreadsheets without timestamps, owners, or status tracking

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates defining and applying a risk treatment process (preventive controls create/process measures to prevent unmanaged risks).

RSK-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documenting and applying a formal risk treatment process (coverage, ownership, and inclusion in project plans and mobile-device risk registers), unlike sibling controls that might measure detection or remediation speed; measurements must verify documented treatment plans and their integration into project planning and responsibility allocation.

RSK-6.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and frequency of formal information security risk assessments across projects and business units (count of projects with documented risk assessments), the count and severity distribution of risks recorded in the risk register, and the percent of identified risks with an assigned owner and documented treatment plan.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control — auditors need counts of assessments, risks, and assignments to show detection and logging; percentages show coverage, and trends show whether identification is improving over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of projects in the project inventory and count of those with a timestamped information security risk assessment artifact; count of risk register entries created per project with severity and assigned owner; percentage of recorded risks with an associated risk treatment plan and due date; time delta between project initiation and risk assessment completion.
data_signals: project inventory records (project_id, start_date, lifecycle stage)
risk assessment artifacts/documents indexed by project_id with creation timestamps
risk register entries (risk_id, project_id, description, severity, likelihood, owner, status)

evidence of assigned responsibilities (RACI or resource allocation records)

segregation of duties (SoD) matrix entries linked to project roles
timestamps of risk acceptance or closure events

Weak: occasional ad-hoc or retrospective narrative notes in project folders without risk register entries, or annual-only assessments that lack project-level linkage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is an information security risk assessment (aims to identify/record risks), which is primarily about detecting and documenting risks rather than preventing or remediating them.

RSK-7.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and timeliness of documented risk treatment: percentage of recorded information security risks (including project-level risks and remote-work site risks) that have a documented treatment plan, an assigned owner, and target completion dates; and the percentage of projects that include documented security risk treatment before project initiation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and assignment (e.g., how many risks have treatment plans/owners), so percentage metrics best express coverage and compliance versus policy; counts and trends supplement with raw volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of risks in the authoritative risk register that have (a) a documented treatment action, (b) an assigned treatment owner, and (c) a target completion date; compute the percent of active projects with an attached security risk assessment and a documented treatment plan prior to project start. Also count remote-work-site risk assessments and the percent where treatment/control evidence exists.

remote-work-site risk assessment records and remediation evidence
risk acceptance / residual risk records
regulatory exceptions or fine notices referenced as risk indicators

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name and intent (information security risk treatment) aim to prevent incidents by documenting and assigning treatment actions before harm occurs.

SAT-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel who have completed required information security awareness training and role-based security education on schedule, plus effectiveness indicators (assessment scores, phishing simulation click/report rates) and the currency of contacts/role-responsibility knowledge tied to training.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of employees who completed mandatory security awareness training within the required interval (e.g., past 12 months); percentage of role-assigned staff with role-based training; distribution of assessment scores and phishing-simulation click/report rates; freshness of key contact lists and % of projects with documented security tasks and trained assignees. Measurements must be linkable to user IDs, training/course IDs, completion timestamps, assessment results, and project/role assignments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Information security awareness, education and training' implies preventive actions to reduce incidents and clarify responsibilities before they occur.

SEA-1.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of remote log-on/authentication events that follow the organization's approved secure log‑on procedures (per policy), plus counts of remote failed log‑ons and account lockouts to show secure log‑on is enforced for remote work sites.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control verifying coverage/compliance (percentage of remote log‑ons using approved secure procedures). Counts and rates (failed attempts, lockouts) are useful secondary measures and trends show changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of remote-origin authentication events that used approved secure log‑on procedures (as defined by policy) over the audit period; count of failed remote log‑on attempts and account lockouts; list of unique remote source IPs/devices that successfully authenticated and whether those sessions met secure log‑on requirements.

inventory of remote access entry points in use (gateways/origins) during the period

SEA-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of systems and infrastructure with correctly configured time synchronization and acceptable clock offset, plus counts of time-drift incidents that affect log timestamp integrity so auditors can verify logs are correlate-able and system events are accurately ordered.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a coverage/compliance control (are systems synchronized?) so percentage (coverage of synchronized hosts within policy threshold) is best; counts (time-drift incidents) and trends (drift over time) are useful secondary measures to show ongoing health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of hosts and infrastructure components with time synchronization enabled and reporting clock offset within the organization-defined threshold (e.g., <= X seconds); count and timestamps of recorded time-drift incidents (offset > threshold) by host; availability/reachability of authoritative time sources and service status for NTP/chrony per host.

list of hosts with NTP/chrony/Windows Time service installed and enabled
service status logs for ntpd/chronyd/w32time (running/stopped) with timestamps
NTP server reachability/latency and response logs
time-drift alert events (offset > policy threshold) with host and timestamp
log ingestion correlation failures or out-of-order event detections tied to timestamp inconsistencies
configuration records showing NTP server entries per host
schedule/compliance records for time synchronization checks or scans

SEA-3.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of projects that include documented information security requirements and formal specification artifacts (risk analysis, assigned responsibilities, and remote-work security considerations) prior to design/release; and count projects missing these artifacts or security ownership assignments.

SEA-4.measurement_goal: Measure the extent to which information security principles are embedded into system engineering and project lifecycles: percentage of active projects that include documented information security requirements and an assigned security owner, count of completed security/architecture reviews per project, and the percentage of engineering roles with segregation-of-duties assignments enforced.
